skills_coverage.notes: JD states no must-have skills — its 'You'll need to have' floor is only the degree-or-experience and relevant-years items; the entire technical stack sits under 'Even better if you have', which stance A excludes. v1.1 derived-musts therefore apply: six requirements derived from the duties section, bundled per sentence. Five are absent outright — no Big Data/Cloud platform, no data pipelines, no semantic layer/dimension tables, no schema/metadata work, no fact tables and no Tableau/Looker (the resume's only reporting is Cognos response-time testing and SQL-driven financial reports, both QA deliverables, and 'reports' alone does not carry a majority-absent bundle). The training-other-developers component of the platform item is genuinely evidenced (QA team onboarding, training videos, mentoring), but it is one component of a majority-absent bundle. The Teradata SQL item is partial, not absent: Teradata itself and query performance tuning are absent, but SQL is hands-on evidenced on Interbase/Oracle for data validation and test-data mining — a different RDBMS is clearly adjacent under the rubric's PostgreSQL-for-MySQL rule, so claiming no keyword and no adjacent equivalent would be false. Five absent + one partial → band 1 by the v1.1 geometry.

education_domain_fit.notes: Bachelor's Degree in Computer Applications. Level meets the JD's Bachelor's ask cleanly. hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's two explicitly stated 'You'll need to have' musts. Degree = met (Bachelor's in Computer Applications satisfies the Bachelor's-OR-experience disjunction outright, and clears 'related' comfortably). Relevant-four-or-more-years = unmet (14 years all QA/testing, zero role-matching dated segments for a Data Engineer JD, reusing the experience_level determination). One clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Aligns with the dataset's No Fit. The six derived skills musts are deliberately NOT in this ledger: the rubric scopes hard_requirements to items the JD explicitly marks as must/required, and v1.1 derives skills musts only to keep skills_coverage defined — a derived item must not fire a veto on a requirement the JD never mandated. Coupling note: the degree disjunction is met via its degree half, so the unmet years item is not double-counting the same evidence.

hesitations: v1.1 relabel of the session-1 record (originally labeled under v1.0, where this JD's absence of stated skill musts left band geometry undefined and skills_coverage was scored 0 as a manual 'essentially no match' call). Under the v1.1 derived-musts rule the same evidence now bands at 1, because the Teradata SQL item resolves to partial rather than absent on the strength of adjacent generic-SQL evidence — the geometry pins any-absent at 1 and reserves 0 for all-absent. The qualitative reading ('essentially no match') still argues for 0; the geometry is followed rather than overridden, since reproducing the old answer by hand is exactly the drift the rule was written to remove. Rule question for the owner: v1.1 does not state whether derived musts enter the hard_requirements ledger; read here as no (see hard_requirements notes) — a yes reading would not change this pair's veto, which the stated years item already fires. Second-order: the derived-musts rule gives no guidance on duties sentences whose named 'skill' is an activity rather than a tool, which is what made the Teradata item's partial-vs-absent call load-bearing for the band.

skills_coverage.notes: Three stated skills musts, all from the 'REQUIRED KNOWLEDGE, SKILLS AND ABILITIES' and 'COMPUTER SKILLS' blocks (neither is marked preferred, so stance A does not exclude them). Excel = partial: the tool is present only as the generic 'MS Office Suite' skills-list entry, while the 'strong' component the JD spells out — pivot tables, VLOOKUP — has no evidence anywhere in the work history. GAAP = covered via adjacency, not keyword: US GAAP is never named, but accrual-basis accounting, journal entries, month-end reconciliations and partnering with external auditors are transferable evidence of the same framework, which the rubric treats as clearly adjacent and caps at mid-band. ERP/MS Office = partial: MS Office is keyword-present, but no ERP is named anywhere and 'accounting software' is the closest the resume comes. Two partials, no absent → band 2 by the geometry (partial caps at 2). The generic literacy/numeracy abilities are tagged other, not skills, so they do not move this band.

experience_level.notes: JD asks a minimum of 5 years accounting experience. education_domain_fit.notes: Bachelor of Arts in Art. Level meets the JD's Bachelor's ask cleanly, but the field is unrelated to the accounting occupation — Art is not accounting-adjacent under any reading, and the JD names its fields narrowly ('Accounting or Finance') with no 'or related' clause to stretch. The band-3 text needs the non-fitting half to be ADJACENT, which this is not; the band-1 criteria text names 'field unrelated to the occupation' explicitly. → 1. Recorded tension: band 1's headline reads 'Neither fits' while its criteria text is disjunctive (unrelated field OR no degree), and here the level half does fit — the criteria text governs, but a level-met/field-unrelated case sits awkwardly between the two bands.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's stated musts. Degree = unmet (BA in Art against 'Bachelors Degree in Accounting or Finance', no related-field clause — clearly unmet, not indeterminate). 5 years accounting = unmet (~3.8 evidenced in role-matching segments, reusing the experience_level determination). Excel, GAAP and ERP/MS Office = indeterminate: each has real but weak evidence a reasonable annotator could defensibly judge either way (generic MS Office Suite for 'strong Excel'; accrual accounting for 'U.S. GAAP'; 'accounting software' for a named ERP). Generic literacy/numeracy abilities = met. Two clear unmet items → 0 — the ledger has no ratio, so the three indeterminates and one met change nothing; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Scope note: the JD's dispositional boilerplate (flexible and adaptable, self-motivated, works professionally with all levels, supports company values, completes the training plan) is deliberately NOT ledgered — the rubric's scope_notes enumerate the ledger's categories as skills, years, degrees, certifications, licenses, visas and location, all resume-evidenceable, and a disposition is none of these.

hesitations: Rule question for the owner: the hard_requirements ledger has no stated rule for dispositional 'must' boilerplate ('Must be flexible and adaptable to change', 'self-motivated'). Read here as out of scope on the strength of the scope_notes category list, because the alternative — ledgering them as indeterminate since a resume cannot evidence a disposition — would fire an indeterminate veto on nearly every pair in the corpus and destroy the gate signal. A yes reading would not change this pair's veto, which the degree and years items already fire. Second: 'Strong MS Excel (Pivot Tables, VLOOKUP, etc.)' was read as one requirement whose parenthetical exemplifies the strength asked, not as a three-component bundle; had it been bundled, Excel present with pivot tables and VLOOKUP absent would be majority-absent → absent → band 1 rather than 2. The rubric's bundle example ('Microservices, Docker, Kubernetes') is a list of coordinate technologies, which this parenthetical is not, but the distinction is doing real band work here.

skills_coverage.notes: Four stated skills musts from the 'Required' block; the 'Desired' block (Bachelor's in CS, Microservices, Kubernetes, MongoDB, SOLR, Agile/Scrum) is excluded by stance A. The Java bundle is the decisive item and it is absent: Java, Spring Boot and Hibernate have no keyword and no adjacent equivalent anywhere — the resume's JavaScript is a different language and must not be read as Java, and its entire stack is C++/C#/graphics (Bentley APIs, ObjectArx, MicroStation, AutoCAD, OpenGL). Only SQL Databases lands, via MySQL/Oracle/MS Access in the tools list. Three of four coordinate technologies absent → majority-absent → absent, which pins the band at 1 regardless of the rest. DevOps is likewise absent: no GIT, Jenkins, JIRA or Confluence, and no CI/CD of any kind — the bmake build scripts are build tooling, not continuous integration. SDLC best practices = partial: design, implementation and testing are hands-on evidenced and bmake covers build, but code reviews, source control, automation and end-to-end traceability have no evidence. Databases = covered at keyword level (Oracle named, MySQL adjacent; the JD's and/or makes any one sufficient), but no work-history evidence of writing or amending queries. Band 1 by the geometry (any absent). Bundle-vs-exemplification reading, applied consistently across this batch: 'Java, Spring Boot, SQL Databases, and Hibernate' is a list of coordinate technologies → bundle, majority rule; 'best practices ... including coding standards, code reviews ...' and 'databases including ... Oracle, SQL, PostgreSQL' are head skills with 'including' exemplars → head skill governs, exemplars set covered/partial.

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement, and a sharp one: the dataset labels this Good Fit, while the rubric puts skills_coverage at the bottom band and fires the veto. The candidate is a genuinely strong senior engineer — 15 years, Microsoft and Adobe, CS degree, MS — who does not have the JD's stack: no Java, no Spring Boot, no Hibernate, no DevOps toolchain. This is the same shape as the rubric's own train 4699 anchor (strong candidate, absent bundle, dataset says Good Fit), which suggests the dataset's labels track general seniority and profession-level match rather than stated-requirement coverage. Worth carrying into the P0 report as the second formal disagreement sample. Second: 'Current' in the Microsoft segment has no end date; read conservatively against the dataset's 2024 pin it is ~12 years, and the 10+ floor is met under any reading, so nothing turns on it.

skills_coverage.notes: The JD states its skills musts outright (the 'Skills' block marks Python/SQL/R/PySpark/Scala 'essential', and the 'Qualifications' block restates SQL, Tableau and Hadoop), so v1.1 derived-musts does not apply — the duties section's HDFS and A/B testing content is not mined for extra requirements. Where the Skills and Qualifications blocks name the same technology the item is merged rather than double-listed; the two genuinely preferred markers ('(Python, Pyspark, Scala, andor R skills are a plus)', 'Tableau or other BI visualization tools would be advantageous') are excluded by stance A, but Tableau survives as a must via its unmarked restatement in Qualifications. Every one of the five requirements is absent: the resume is an excavator operator and commercial diver — no keyword and no adjacent equivalent for any programming language, ETL tooling, Hadoop, Tableau, or patient-journey analytics. Its only computing content is 'Excel, Microsoft Word, Access, Power Point and QuickBooks' in a duties list and a Microsoft Software Administrator program, none of which is adjacent to a data-analysis stack. All absent → band 0 by the geometry (all-absent), which is the one case the geometry reserves for essentially no match.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's stated musts. hesitations: Straightforward pair — the rubric, the aggregate and the dataset all agree, and no judgment call here was close. The one recorded interpretation: the no-sponsorship clause is ledgered as indeterminate rather than skipped, on the ground that the rubric's scope_notes name visas explicitly; it changes nothing here (seven items are already unmet) but the same clause could be the sole non-met item on a stronger pair, where it would fire an indeterminate veto on a resume that simply never mentions work authorization. Flagging as a rule question the owner may want to settle before the gate config is written.

skills_coverage.notes: The JD names exactly one skills requirement, and it arrives embedded in a years clause: software engineering 'using C and Java'. Per the rubric's boundary rule the quantity half (15+ years) is scored under experience_level and only the skill's presence and evidence strength are judged here. Both components land at the floor: Java appears verbatim and C++ is clearly adjacent to C, so neither component is absent → covered. But the evidence is keyword-level only and sits in the resume's trailing auto-generated skills blob ('AutoCad, C++, cable, cash register, CCNA, ... Java, meetings, access, ...'); no work-history entry shows either language in use — every dated segment is network operations, Layer 2/3 configuration, UPS maintenance and electrical design. hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's 'What you will need to have' block. C-and-Java skill = met (both components at least keyword/adjacent, reusing the skills_coverage determination — the ledger asks presence, not strength). 15+ years software engineering = unmet; 10+ years leading engineering teams = unmet; 5+ years financial services or payments = unmet — all three reuse the experience_level determination that no dated segment is role-matching. Executive-leadership communication = indeterminate: pre-sales demonstrations to Fortune 500, military and ISP customers plus customer training are real client-facing communication and adjacent to the ask, but nothing evidences communication with executive leadership specifically — defensibly judgeable either way, which is precisely band 3's reservation. hesitations: skills_coverage lands at 3 for a network engineer applying to a Software Engineering Manager role, which is the highest band any of the No Fit pairs has drawn so far and is worth the owner's attention. It is not a misapplication — the JD names only C and Java as technologies, and both are traceable — but the credit comes entirely from the flat keyword blob this corpus's resume builder appends to every document ('AutoCad, C++, cable, cash register, CCNA, ...'), a list that reads as machine-extracted rather than authored. Band 3 explicitly accepts skills-list mentions, so the rubric is being followed; the observation is that a corpus artifact can hand out the passing floor cheaply, and that JDs naming few technologies concentrate the whole 0.5 weight on that thin evidence. The weights absorb it here (experience_level 1 and the veto carry the pair to a gate), but if this recurs it is rubric-revision material: a possible rule that a skills-list mention with no work-history corroboration caps below the floor. Recording rather than overriding — the geometry gave a defined answer and hand-correcting it is the drift the v1.1 rule exists to prevent.

skills_coverage.notes: Three stated skills musts from the REQUIREMENTS block. Regulatory-climate knowledge and regulator negotiation are likewise absent — the ISO 9001 internal auditor and NEBOSH health-and-safety certificates are compliance training, not adjacent to negotiating with state and local energy regulators. Office/computer skills are covered at keyword level ('Proficient in working with Microsoft Excel, Word, Power Point & Outlook'). Two absent → band 1 by the geometry. The JD's substantial technical content — short-circuit, arc flash, cable ampacity, grounding, voltage drop, NEC and IEEE/ANSI standards — sits in RESPONSIBILITIES and is deliberately not mined: derived-musts is a fallback for JDs that state no skills musts, and this JD states three.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the REQUIREMENTS block. Degree = met (BE Electrical & Electronics is a B.S.-level Electrical Engineering degree, reusing the education_domain_fit determination). hesitations: Anomaly gate reason recorded: this resume is partly corrupt and partly template residue. Both work-history date fields read '01/1to01/1' rather than real dates, which is what forces experience_level to band 1 on evidentiary grounds before content is even considered; and the certifications block contains the stray line 'MR.Jessica Claire RESUME MOBILE: (555) 432-1000.', which is resume-builder boilerplate carrying a different person's name and a placeholder phone number. Neither defect changes the outcome — the pair fails on six unmet requirements independently — but a screening agent asked to cite evidence spans on this document will encounter unparseable dates and a contaminated identity field, so it is exactly the kind of scenario the P2 gate-integrity work should see. Recorded rather than cleaned: the reference set should reflect the corpus as it is.

skills_coverage.notes: A 666-character JD carrying exactly three requirements, one of which is a skills must: advanced GAAP knowledge plus accounting software. Both components are absent. GAAP is never named and nothing in the resume is adjacent to it — there is no accounting content of any kind, no journal entries, no reconciliations, no month-end close; the candidate is an information-systems analyst. Accounting software is likewise absent: the tooling on offer is Microsoft Access, Excel, Project, SharePoint, SQL Server, Tableau, Visual Studio, Spotfire, Visio, CRM, TFS and AWS, and none of these is accounting software or adjacent to it (Excel is a spreadsheet, and the JD names it nowhere). Both components absent → absent → band 0 by the geometry, the all-absent case. Structural note for the report: this JD names a single skills requirement, so the entire 0.5 weight of the rubric's heaviest dimension rests on one determination — a short JD gives the dimension a very narrow evidentiary base, which is a property of the corpus rather than a defect in this label.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's three stated requirements — all three unmet, which makes this the cleanest veto in the batch so far. Degree = unmet (Electronics & Communication and Information Systems against 'Accounting or Finance or related field'; not a related field, so clearly unmet rather than indeterminate — band 3 is for items judgeable either way, and this is not one). GAAP and accounting software = unmet (absent, reusing the skills_coverage determination). Three clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Agrees with the dataset's No Fit.

hesitations: Anomaly gate reason recorded for corrupt date fields: four of the five work-history entries read '01/1-01/1' where dates belong, the same corruption seen in train 2189, which suggests a systematic date-parsing failure in this corpus rather than a one-off bad row. It does not change this label — the pair fails on all three requirements on content alone — but it matters for the agent evaluation ahead: a screening agent asked to count evidenced years on these documents has no dates to count, and how it behaves then (refuse, guess, or fall back to the Summary self-claim the rubric excludes) is exactly what the gate should catch. The Business Data Analyst Intern entry also nests a second employer inside it (Baby deedee IT Admin Intern, Oct'16–Dec'16), so even the segment boundaries are unreliable here.

skills_coverage.notes: This JD states exactly one must, and it marks it explicitly: 'Must have strong exp in Omni Channels.' Omni Channel is absent — the word appears nowhere and nothing in the resume is adjacent to omnichannel customer engagement. The nearest content is genuinely interesting but does not reach it: the candidate built integrations from Salesforce and Pardot (a marketing automation platform) at TransCanada and worked with brand and marketing teams at Barclays. That is Salesforce-platform and marketing-data work, not Omni-Channel routing, so it is a neighbouring product area rather than a transferable equivalent of the named skill. One requirement, absent → all-absent → band 0. Recorded structural tension, because it is doing all the work here: v1.1's derived-musts rule fires only when a JD states NO must-have skills, so a JD stating exactly one thin must — as this 716-character posting does — never reaches the fallback, and the whole 0.5 weight rests on a single phrase. The result is a band 0 ('essentially no match') for a candidate with real Salesforce and marketing-data experience, which the duties section would have surfaced had the rule been allowed to run. This is the one-thin-must gap, a sibling of the no-musts gap v1.1 closed.

determinations.requirement: Strong experience in Omni Channels

experience_level.notes: The JD states no year count, so proximity is judged against the role it describes: a Business Analyst capturing functional and technical requirements. Dated segments are complete and well-formed here — Database Application Developer 02/2011–04/2013, Sr Database App/BI Developer 05/2013–10/2015, Sr ETL Developer 01/2016–08/2016, Sr Data Engineer/Architect 08/2016–current — about 13 years, all at senior level by the back half. The titles are engineering, not BA, but the rubric matches on role OR duties, and the duties match repeatedly and across every segment: analyzing user needs and software requirements to determine design feasibility, contributing to requirements gathering and design meetings, developing requirements for system modifications, assessing project scope, reviewing project requirements to identify customer expectations, developing technical and functional specifications, and partnering with project management teams on scope and timelines. That is the BA core, evidenced in dated segments. The real gap is that requirements work is a component of these roles rather than the job itself, and no segment touches Omni Channels. Clear relevant experience in role-matching segments with a real gap → 3.

education_domain_fit.notes: BSc in Electrical Electronics Engineering (University of Leicester). The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask scored is the role-implied one: bachelor's level for a technical Business Analyst. Level meets it cleanly. Field is engineering rather than business analysis or information systems — related-rather-than-core for this occupation, but genuinely adjacent, because the role is an explicitly technical BA capturing 'functional, and technical requirements' and documenting 'technical dependencies and system interactions', which an engineering education supports. Level met + field adjacent → 3, not sub-graded. Consistency note: an engineering degree drew a 1 against the accounting JDs (train 3559, train 175) and a 3 here, and the difference is deliberate — accounting is a domain-credentialed occupation where an unrelated field genuinely does not fit, whereas technical business analysis draws from general technical education, and the rubric requires fit to be judged JD-relative rather than against a fixed yardstick.

hard_requirements.notes: The ledger has exactly one item, because the JD marks exactly one: 'Must have strong exp in Omni Channels' = unmet (absent, reusing the skills_coverage determination). One clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Everything else in this posting is a responsibility rather than a requirement — define scope, capture and communicate requirements to stakeholders, document technical dependencies, work with QA on testing, partner with the PM on sprint planning, track Jira stories — and the ledger is scoped to items the JD explicitly marks must/required, so none of it is ledgered. The onsite preference ('Preference will be given to someone who can go onsite') is a preference, not a requirement, and is excluded on stance A. Agrees with the dataset's No Fit.

hesitations: insufficient_evidence gate reason recorded: skills_coverage — the 0.5-weight dimension — is scored from a single four-word requirement in a 716-character JD, and both the aggregate and the veto turn entirely on that one determination. The inputs are rubric-defined, unlike the train 596 v1.0 case, so this is not the no-musts gap; it is the adjacent one. A JD with exactly one stated skills must cannot reach v1.1's derived-musts fallback, so the duties section — which names Jira, sprint planning, requirements documentation and, in the title, Salesforce Marketing Cloud — is never consulted, and a candidate with Salesforce and Pardot integration experience scores 'essentially no match'. Rubric-revision material for the owner: consider whether derived-musts should trigger on a threshold (no stated skills musts, or fewer than N) rather than only on zero, and whether a JD title naming a platform is a requirement source. Recording rather than overriding — the geometry gave a defined answer, and hand-correcting it is the drift v1.1 exists to prevent. Second, smaller: the dataset's No Fit agrees with the outcome here, so nothing about this pair's label is in dispute — only the route the rubric took to it.

skills_coverage.notes: One stated skills must, and the JD bundles it into a single 'required' sentence: advanced Excel, engineering drawing practices, manufacturing ERP systems and practices, and structured BOMs. All four components are absent — this is not a majority call. Excel appears nowhere in a resume that otherwise lists tooling exhaustively (Eclipse, Visual Studio, QTP, GIT, Jagacy, C/C++/Java/C#, Oracle 10g, MySQL, DB2, MongoDB, Docker, Hadoop, HTML5/CSS/JavaScript). Engineering drawing practices and structured BOMs have no keyword and nothing adjacent — the candidate has never worked in manufacturing. Manufacturing ERP is absent too, and the near-miss is worth naming: the resume's 'ORACLE 10g' is the relational database, not Oracle ERP, and reading it as coverage of 'manufacturing ERP systems & practices' would be a keyword collision rather than adjacency. All components absent → absent → band 0, the all-absent case. Fenestration industry experience is marked 'a strong plus' and excluded by stance A.

determinations.requirement: Advanced Excel, Engineering drawing practices, manufacturing ERP systems & practices, structured BOMs

experience_level.notes: JD asks 10 years of progressive experience with at least 5 of those in a customer-facing software environment. The years clause is generic — 'progressive experience', not 10 years of ERP configuration — so role-matching is judged against software work broadly. Dated segments: Software Engineer at Jacobs 06/2014–06/2016 (~2 yrs, mainframe modernization tooling, automation, CI) and Software Intern 11/2013–04/2014 (~0.4 yr) ≈ 2.4 years of role-matching experience. The Online Tutor segment 06/2012–present teaches C/C++/Java and SAT/ACT preparation; it is tutoring rather than software engineering practice, so it does not accrue. Real relevant experience in role-matching segments, but the gap is large — roughly 2.4 years against a 10-year floor, and under half of even the 5-year customer-facing sub-floor, with 'Customer facing' appearing only as a self-described skill rather than a duty in any dated segment. Clear relevant experience with a real gap → 3. Recorded observation: the band-3 text does not scale with gap size, so a 7.6-year shortfall bands identically to train 3559's ~1.2-year shortfall — the rubric buys stability by not sub-grading proximity, and the veto is what separates them.

education_domain_fit.notes: Bachelor's in Computer Science Engineering (Kakatiya University, 3.8) plus a Master of Engineering in Computer Science (University of Cincinnati, 3.9). The JD asks a BA/BS in an 'applicable major' — deliberately open — for a role whose substance is creating and maintaining ERP product configuration software applications. Computer science is applicable and core to that, not merely adjacent: the work is software development against an ERP platform. Level meets the ask with a master's on top, field squarely within the occupation → 5. The JD-relative rule matters here — this is judged against a software-configuration occupation, and the fact that the candidate lacks the manufacturing domain knowledge is scored under skills_coverage, not imported into education.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the Minimum Qualifications block. Degree = met (BS Computer Science Engineering and ME Computer Science both sit in an applicable major for a software configuration role, reusing the education_domain_fit determination; the JD's disjunction also allows an equivalent combination of education and experience, and the degree half passes outright). Skills bundle = unmet (all four components absent, reusing the skills_coverage determination). 10 years progressive = unmet (~2.4 evidenced). 5 years customer-facing collaborative software = unmet (no dated segment evidences customer-facing work; 'L2 issue management and Customer facing' is a self-described skill-list entry, not a duty in a dated role). Three clear unmet items → 0 — one met item buys nothing, the ledger has no ratio; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Agrees with the dataset's No Fit.

hesitations: The Professional Profile opens 'Highly motivated Sales Associate with extensive customer service and sales experience' on what is unambiguously a software engineer's resume — the same two sentences appear verbatim atop train 5063's graphics-engineer resume, so this is resume-builder boilerplate stamped across the corpus rather than a claim by this candidate. Deliberately NOT recorded as an anomaly gate reason, and the line drawn for the batch is worth stating: garbled or unparseable content (the '01/1to01/1' date corruption in train 2189 and train 175) gets the anomaly flag, while systematic template boilerplate that is merely wrong about the candidate does not — it is noise the corpus applies uniformly, not a defect in this document. It is a live hazard for the agent evaluation, though: a screening agent that reads the Summary line will conclude this candidate is in sales, and the rubric's exclusion of Summary self-claims in favour of dated segments is exactly what protects against it. Worth carrying into the P0 report as a corpus-artifact note.

skills_coverage.notes: The JD states one skills must — SQL proficiency plus quantitative analysis and the ability to deep-dive large data for actionable insight — and every component is covered with hands-on evidence in dated and undated work entries alike. SQL is not merely listed but exercised: complex PL/SQL queries written against Teradata, SSIS packages designed to load a warehouse, long-running SQL and PL/SQL tuned for performance, DDL/DML written to improve data quality. Quantitative analysis is likewise evidenced in role duties — statistical analytics features of Tableau, time series analysis, financial modeling and trend mapping, statistical calculation across campaign datasets. The deep-dive-to-insight component is the substance of every role here. All covered, all hands-on → band 5. This is the first top-band skills score in the set, and it lands on a pair the dataset calls No Fit — see hesitations. The dispositional items in the same Experience block (outcome-driven approach, exceptional communication, a drive to understand core issues, excels at dealing with ambiguity) are not skills requirements and do not move the band.

determinations.requirement: Proficient in SQL and quantitative analysis; deep dive into large data, draw insights and actionable conclusions

experience_level.notes: JD asks 2-5 years in data and/or financial analytics — a modest floor this candidate's career plainly clears. But the rubric admits evidenced years from dated entries only, and three of the four work entries carry corrupt date fields: Tableau Developer/Data Analyst at Alteryx reads '01/1toPresent', and both the Sr BI Consultant and Business Intelligence Analyst entries read '01/1to01/1'. The single parseable segment is Business Analyst 08/2016–11/2016, roughly three months, and it is role-matching — data analyst and business system analyst duties, data mapping, data analysis, dimensional modeling. So there is at least one role-matching dated segment, which rules out band 1, but the evidenced total is ~0.25 years against a 2-year floor. Clear relevant experience in a role-matching segment with a real gap → 3. The band understates this candidate, and the reason is evidentiary rather than substantive: the years are almost certainly there and the corpus has destroyed the dates that would prove it. Scoring what is evidenced rather than what is inferable is the rubric's explicit stance, so the 3 stands and the distortion is recorded.

education_domain_fit.notes: BS in Chemical Engineering (Oklahoma State). The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's level in a quantitative field for a data and financial analytics role. Level meets it. Field is chemical engineering — not data science, statistics, economics or finance, but a heavily quantitative engineering discipline whose training in modeling, statistics and process analysis is genuinely transferable to analytics work. Related-rather-than-core → 3, not sub-graded. Note the sample's occupation bucket for this pair reads 'hr/admin', which the taxonomy's first-match-wins keyword scan produced; the rubric requires fit to be judged against the occupation the JD actually describes, which is data/financial analytics, and that is what is scored here. The bucket label is sampling metadata, not a rubric input.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's Experience block. SQL and quantitative analysis = met (covered with hands-on evidence, reusing the skills_coverage determination). Attention to detail / processes to remove human error = met (mapping-level data auditing and error handling, data scrubbing with validation checks during staging, explicit data-quality work). 2-5 years in data/financial analytics = INDETERMINATE, not unmet: the resume evidences an extensive analytics career across four roles, but three of the four have corrupt date fields, so the years cannot be counted either way — this is precisely 'cannot decide', which is what band 3 is reserved for, and calling it unmet would punish the candidate for a corpus defect while calling it met would credit years the document does not evidence. No item clearly unmet, one indeterminate → 3; the soft veto fires in its indeterminate mode → force_gate only, no aggregate cap. This is the first pair in the set to reach the indeterminate branch of the veto rather than the unmet branch, which is worth noting: the two branches were designed to give gate_expected a natural sub-classification, and the mechanism works here exactly as intended — a human is asked to resolve an ambiguity rather than to review a rejection.

hesitations: The sharpest owner-vs-dataset disagreement in the set so far, and in the opposite direction from train 5063: the dataset labels this No Fit, while the rubric returns a weighted mean of 4.0 with only an indeterminate veto. The JD is an analyst role wanting SQL, quantitative analysis and data deep-dives; the resume is a career Tableau/BI data analyst with hands-on SQL, PL/SQL, Teradata, statistical analytics and financial modeling. On stated requirements this is a good match, and the disagreement does not look like a rubric artifact — it looks like the dataset label is wrong, or is tracking something other than requirement coverage (perhaps the JD's rideshare-marketplace domain, which the candidate lacks). Recommend the owner review this pair specifically; taken with train 5063 and the train 4699 anchor it suggests the dataset's three classes are not a requirement-coverage judgment at all, which is a material finding for the P0 report and for how the dataset label is used as a base layer. anomaly recorded for the '01/1' date corruption across three of four work entries — the same defect seen in train 2189 and train 175, now clearly systematic. insufficient_evidence recorded because experience_level is scored from ~0.25 years of parseable dates while the candidate's actual tenure is unknowable from the document; the resulting 3 is an artifact of the corruption, and had the dates survived this dimension would likely be a 5 and the pair a clear advance.

skills_coverage.notes: A 285-character JD that is nothing but a requirements list, and all four requirements are absent against a career accountant's resume. Cucumber, BDD and Selenium: no keyword, nothing adjacent — the resume's tooling is Tally, Oracle 8i, Developer 2000, MS Office and the Adobe suite. Test Plan/Test Case/Test Scenario development: all three components absent. Data requests from test cases and data mining: absent — the MIS reports and cost/variance analysis are financial reporting, not test-data mining, and reading them as adjacent would stretch adjacency past transferability. The second item deserves its reasoning stated, because it is the only one with a near-miss: 'testing Financial Domain applications' pairs a skill (application testing) with a domain qualifier (finance), and the candidate has the domain in depth — nine years of accounting, an MBA in Finance — but none of the skill. The requirement is read as atomic rather than decomposed: the domain qualifier is not itself a skill component, so possessing it does not make the requirement partially covered when the skill it qualifies is wholly absent. Decomposing it would have produced 'partial' and lifted the band from 0 to 1; the reading follows the same head-skill logic applied to the parenthetical and 'including' lists elsewhere in this batch. All four absent → band 0.

determinations.requirement: Cucumber BDD + Selenium UI Automation experience
Excellent knowledge and experience in testing Financial Domain applications
Hands-on experience in Test Plan, Test Case and Test Scenario development
Experience creating data requests based on test cases, and data mining

experience_level.notes: The JD states no year count, so proximity is judged against the role it describes: a QA automation engineer testing financial applications. The resume's dated segments are substantial and well-formed for once — Account Assistant 09/2001–02/2006, Accountant 03/2006–04/2007, Accountant 2009–08/2011, Accountant 09/2011–07/2015, roughly thirteen years — but every one of them is accounting: reconciliations, payroll, receivables, budget preparation, MIS reporting, stock taking. Zero role-matching segments for a testing role; the word test appears nowhere in a work-history duty. Real and lengthy experience, entirely off-occupation → band 1, the same shape as train 596 and train 400.

education_domain_fit.notes: MBA in Finance (Vidyasagar University, 2006); the second education entry is corrupt, its degree field reading literally 'Select...:'. The JD asks for no degree, so the ask is role-implied: technical bachelor's-level education for a QA automation role. Level is met and then some by a master's. Field is the problem — finance is unrelated to the software testing occupation. The tempting move is to call it adjacent because the JD wants financial-domain applications tested, but the rubric forecloses it: industry verticals are excluded from this dimension by owner decision (option A), and finance is exactly such a vertical. Domain here means occupational/functional field, and this JD's occupation is software QA, to which an MBA in Finance is unrelated. Field unrelated → 1. The candidate's financial domain knowledge is real and does bear on the JD's second requirement; the rubric's structure places that consideration under skills_coverage, where it was weighed and found not to cover a testing skill.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over all four of the JD's requirements — every one unmet, reusing the skills_coverage determinations. Four clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Agrees with the dataset's No Fit. Interpretation recorded, because this JD tests the ledger's scope language: the rubric scopes hard_requirements to items the JD 'explicitly marks as must/required', and this posting contains neither word — it is a bare list headed 'Skills -' with each line phrased as 'Excellent knowledge and experience in ...', 'Hands-on experience in ...'. Read here as stated requirements. The alternative literal reading would leave the ledger empty, which scores 5 by the criteria's own logic (no item unmet, none indeterminate) and would hand a met veto and no gate to a candidate matching none of the JD — plainly not the rule's intent. The 'explicitly marks' language exists to separate requirements from duties and nice-to-haves, not to demand a particular verb; where a JD's entire content is its requirements list, the list is the ledger.

hesitations: anomaly recorded: this resume is materially corrupt. Placeholder tokens sit where words belong — 'Responsible for payable are XXX on time', 'liabilities are XXX on time', 'computer spreadsheets/XXXbases' — the Summary carries an unfilled template field twice ('Flexible[Job Title]who adapts seamlessly to constantly evolving accounting processes'), that same sentence is duplicated verbatim, there is mojibake ('accountantÆs book entries'), and one education entry's degree field reads 'Select...:' where a degree name belongs. Two employment stints (Auto1 Kuwait Aug'07–Dec'08, Digital Vish Computers Sep'00–Sep'01) are buried inside other entries' duty text rather than carrying their own date fields. None of it changes the outcome — four requirements are absent on content — but this document is a good P2 gate-integrity case: an agent asked to cite spans here will find placeholders and a duplicated identity-free Summary. Recorded rather than cleaned, per the stance that the reference set should reflect the corpus as it is. Second: the ledger-scope reading above is an interpretation the owner may want to ratify, since it generalises to any terse skills-list JD in this corpus.

skills_coverage.notes: Scored against profile 1, the posting's title role — 'Software Data Engineer (ScalaPython)' — see hesitations on the two-profile structure. Python is covered with hands-on evidence across dated segments (pandas automation saving 7 hours/week, SQL and pandas data manipulation); Scala is absent, but the requirement reads 'Scala andor Python', so Python alone satisfies it. The tooling line is covered: Jenkins and S3 appear verbatim, GIT is clearly adjacent to GitLab and Bitbucket (same version-control capability), and RDS is adjacent via Postgres/MySQL/SQL-Server plus Redshift — no component absent, though the evidence is skills-list keyword level. Infrastructure-as-Code is partial: the cloud data platform half is hands-on and substantial (EC2, S3, EMR, Athena, CloudWatch, Redshift and Snowflake schema work), but Infrastructure-as-Code itself is absent — no Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi or equivalent — so some components present, some absent. The Grafana/Prometheus item is absent: both named tools are missing, and while the resume does monitor data loads in production (Splunk, PagerDuty, troubleshooting production data-load failures, daily load-status email) and builds dashboards in Tableau, those are BI dashboards and log/alert tooling rather than the ingestion-monitoring dashboards named — two of three components absent → majority-absent. One absent determination → band 1 by the geometry, notwithstanding two covered items and a genuine partial.

determinations.requirement: Programming in Scala and/or Python
Cloud data platform development and deployment using Infrastructure-as-Code
Operational dashboards to monitor data ingestion using Grafana, Prometheus
Other technologies: GitLab, Bitbucket, Jenkins, RDS, S3

experience_level.notes: JD asks 3+ years programming in Scala and/or Python. Dated segments are complete and well-formed: Data Analyst/Production Support 05/2018–03/2019 (~0.8 yr), Data Analyst at Capital One 04/2019–08/2021 (~2.3 yrs), Data Analyst/Data Engineer 09/2021–current (~3 yrs) — roughly 6 years of data engineering and analysis. Role-matching for a Python data-engineering ask is satisfied directly: Python is named in the duties of the current segment (pandas automation) and of the 2018–2019 segment (SQL and pandas data manipulation), so the years accrue in segments whose duties match the skill rather than merely coexisting with it. Even counting only the two segments that name Python, the evidenced total (~3.8 yrs) clears the 3-year floor; counting the Capital One segment's scripting and performance-tuning work it is comfortably past. Meets/exceeds → 5. The Summary's '3.5 years of vast experience' self-claim is excluded on principle and is not needed — the dated segments carry it.

education_domain_fit.notes: BS in Electrical, Electronics and Communications Engineering (2015) plus a Management Information Systems credential (2017) whose degree level the document never states — the entry reads 'Education and Training:Management Information Systems' with no 'Bachelor'/'Master' prefix, unlike the ECE entry. The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level technical education for a data engineering role. Level is met by the ECE bachelor's regardless of how the MIS entry resolves, so the missing level does not change the band. Field: ECE is engineering and MIS is information systems — both related to data engineering, neither core to it (MIS carries a business-systems orientation rather than a computer-science one). Level met + field related-rather-than-core → 3, not sub-graded. Consistent with the reading applied to train 596's Computer Applications degree.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over profile 1's requirements. 3+ years Scala/Python = met (Python hands-on across ~3.8 years of role-matching dated segments, reusing the experience_level determination; the and/or disjunction makes Scala's absence irrelevant). Tooling line = met (no component absent, reusing the skills_coverage determination). Grafana/Prometheus operational dashboards = unmet (majority-absent bundle). Infrastructure-as-Code cloud platform = indeterminate: the cloud data platform work is real and hands-on while IaC itself is absent, and a reasonable annotator could judge a partial like this either way — it is not majority-absent, so the unmet rule does not reach it. Chicago on-site / willing-to-relocate = indeterminate: location sits squarely in the rubric's ledger categories, the JD marks it emphatically ('local candidates or those willing to relocate only!'), and the resume places the candidate in Reston, VA with no statement of relocation willingness — cannot decide, so recorded rather than assumed, consistent with the visa handling in train 901. One clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Direction agrees with the dataset's No Fit.

hesitations: Structural issue the rubric does not cover: this posting advertises TWO roles ('looking to grow their data engineering organization with two engineers ... Please see the details for both profiles below'), each with its own requirement list, and a candidate satisfies the JD by matching either one. The reference standard needs a single determination, so profile 1 was scored on the ground that the posting's title names it — 'Software Data Engineer (ScalaPython)'. This is recorded rather than resolved because it happens not to matter here: profile 2 (3+ years Python; Spark/Kafka/Delta Lake; batch and streaming ETL; EKS/Kubernetes, MSK, Databricks, Flink) yields the same band 1 and the same unmet veto — Python covered, Kafka/Delta Lake/Databricks/Flink absent, streaming ETL partial against batch-only pipeline work, with EMR at best adjacent to Spark. Both routes agree, so the pair's label is stable under either reading; a JD where the profiles diverge would need an owner ruling. Second, the profiles' own labels are internally inconsistent — profile 2 is headed 'Software Engineer (Scala)' but its first requirement is '3+ years experience programming in Python', with Scala appearing nowhere in its list. Third: the resume duplicates whole duty sentences (the 'Writing SQL scripts ... Organized and facilitated sprint planning' block appears twice, and 'Responsible for loading, extracting and validation of client data' three times). No anomaly flag — the dates are intact and nothing is unparseable, so this is redundancy rather than the corruption seen in train 2189, 175, 970 and 1089.

skills_coverage.notes: Nine stated skills musts and the DevOps core of every one of them is missing. AWS is the decisive absence: the JD names it twice and marks it explicitly required ('experience with AWS is required'), enumerating nine services, and not one appears — the resume's only cloud reference is descriptive ('web-based, cloud computing software for physicians'). Chef/Puppet, the APM tools, and the log-analysis/monitoring tools are absent outright, as is always-up IT operations knowledge. The Linux item is absent on the majority rule rather than flatly: Linux and UNIX appear in the operating-systems list, but the requirement's substance — administration, monitoring, reliability and security of high-traffic online properties — has no evidence, so five of six components are missing and a keyword in an OS list does not carry it. Two items are genuinely covered: scripting is hands-on (Perl and Python used to build test automation frameworks against Qualcomm processors), and MySQL is keyword-present. CI/CD is partial: Git is listed, and the JD's 'such as' phrasing admits it as one of the named tools, but nothing evidences CI/CD practice — no pipelines, builds or deployments — and Jenkins and Artifactory are both absent. Six absent determinations → band 1. The Preferred Qualifications block (AEM, React/NodeJS, NoSQL, Agile) is excluded by stance A.

Operations and DevOps on the AWS technology stack (EC2, S3, IAM, RDS, CloudFront, CloudWatch, Lambda, CloudFormation, CDK)
Automation/configuration management using Chef, Puppet or an equivalent

Working experience with MySQL
Wide variety of open source technologies and cloud services (AWS required)
Knowledge of best practices and IT operations in an always-up, always-available service

experience_level.notes: The JD stacks two four-year floors: Linux/Unix administration and AWS DevOps operations. All four work entries carry corrupt date fields reading '01/1to01/1', so no segment is dated and no years can be evidenced — the same corruption seen in train 2189, 175, 970 and 1089. Content is decisive independently: the roles are Software Engineer (test automation for Windows Phone display drivers), Software Engineer (web healthcare development in Java/JSP/PL-SQL), and Assistant Systems Engineer (Siebel CRM configuration and testing). None is a DevOps, SRE, or systems administration role; there are no role-matching segments for either floor. The Summary's '5+ Years of experience in the field of Software development and testing' is an undated self-claim, excluded on principle, and claims software development rather than the operations experience asked for. No role-matching dated segments → band 1.

education_domain_fit.notes: B.Tech in Computer Science (Biju Pattnaik University, 8.1/10) and MS in Computer Science (San Diego State, 4.0). The JD asks a minimum of a BS in computer science or related fields; the bachelor's is exactly that and the master's exceeds it. Level meets the stated ask and field is squarely within the software/DevOps occupation — computer science is core here, not adjacent → 5. As in train 2189, a top-band education sits beside bottom-band skills and experience: the candidate is well educated for this profession and has not done this job. The 0.2 weight keeps that in proportion.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the Minimum Qualifications block. Degree = met (BS-level computer science, reusing the education_domain_fit determination). Scripting = met (Perl/Python hands-on); MySQL = met (keyword). CI/CD tools = indeterminate: Git is present and the JD's own list names it, but no CI/CD practice is evidenced and the two genuine CI/CD tools named are absent — defensibly judgeable either way, which is band 3's reservation. Unmet, each reusing a skills_coverage determination: Linux/Unix administration (majority-absent), AWS operations and DevOps (absent, and explicitly marked required), Chef/Puppet configuration management, APM tools, log analysis and monitoring tools, open-source/cloud breadth with AWS required, and always-up IT operations knowledge. Seven clear unmet → 0 — three met items buy nothing, the ledger has no ratio; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Excluded as dispositional or availability boilerplate: 'Excellent communication and collaboration skills and proven ability to work as part of a team' and 'Occasional travel may be required'.

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement: the dataset says Potential Fit, the rubric returns 1.8 with an unmet veto. The disagreement is explicable and, on stated requirements, the rubric looks right — this is a capable software engineer with two computer science degrees and hands-on Perl/Python, applying to a DevOps role that asks four years each of Linux administration and AWS operations, and he has neither, nor any of the Chef/Puppet/APM/logging toolchain. 'Potential Fit' is a fair description of the person; it is not a description of requirement coverage, which is what this rubric measures. Consistent with train 5063 and the train 4699 anchor, the dataset's classes continue to look like a judgment about candidate calibre and profession rather than about the JD's stated musts. anomaly recorded on two grounds: all four work entries have '01/1to01/1' corrupt dates, and the first 'Software Engineer' entry is not a job at all — it contains San Diego State coursework and student projects (Fall 2012, Spring 2012, a class group project, an honors note about semester rank) presented under a work-history title, so the document's segment boundaries misrepresent academic work as employment. That matters for the agent evaluation: an agent counting years from titles here would credit student projects as professional experience, which is exactly the error the rubric's dated-role-matching-segments rule is built to prevent.

skills_coverage.notes: A genuinely close pair, and the band turns on one component. Seven of eight skills musts are covered and most are hands-on: pipeline operationalization, Python/PySpark, Hadoop/Spark, and data hygiene are all evidenced in dated duties (Spark jobs, PySpark on Spark MLlib, row-count/schema/hash-key data quality checks); CI/CD is covered at keyword level via Git and Jenkins with deployment evidenced. The Azure item is covered by adjacency rather than keyword and deserves its reasoning stated: this is an Azure Data Engineer role naming Azure Databricks and Azure Data Factory, and the candidate is entirely AWS — but Spark itself is hands-on, AWS EMR is managed Spark and so clearly adjacent to Azure Databricks, and Oozie/Sqoop orchestration is adjacent to Azure Data Factory. That is transferable-capability evidence, exactly what the rubric's PostgreSQL-for-MySQL rule admits, so no component is absent → covered, with adjacency capping the strength. Software engineering fundamentals is likewise covered via adjacency — algorithm theory and data structures are never named, but Hive join/bucketing/partition performance work and breaking large-scale requirements into system specifications are real engineering practice. The one partial decides the band: 'relational and non-relational, NoSQL databases' asks both halves, and while relational is hands-on and deep (Teradata, SQL Server, Snowflake, Hive), NoSQL is absent — no document, key-value, column-family or graph store anywhere; Kafka is streaming and HDFS is a filesystem, neither is a NoSQL database. Some components present, some absent → partial → caps the band at 2, notwithstanding seven covered items. This is the geometry working as designed rather than a misfire: a hard cap on any partial is what keeps the band from being a ratio.

determinations.requirement: Experience with Azure Databricks, Spark, and Azure Data Factory
Experience operationalizing production pipelines
Software development using Python / PySpark
Software engineering fundamentals, including algorithm theory and data structures
CI/CD tools to build and deploy software systems
Relational and non-relational, NoSQL databases
Hadoop or Spark
Data hygiene routines and models

experience_level.notes: The JD stacks six floors, the largest being 4 years of Python/PySpark development and 4 years of software engineering fundamentals. Dated segments are complete and well-formed: Big Data Engineer at Verizon 01/2014–07/2017 (~3.5 yrs), Data Scientist Intern 09/2018–12/2018 (~0.3 yr), Data Engineer at Avanade 08/2019–04/2020 (~0.7 yr), Data Engineer at Avanade 04/2020–current (~4+ yrs) — roughly 8.5 years, every one of them a data engineering or data science role. Role-matching is direct rather than inferred: PySpark is named in the Verizon duties and Spark work runs through both Avanade segments, so the Python/PySpark years accrue in segments whose duties match the skill. Even excluding the internship, the evidenced total is roughly double the 4-year floor, and every smaller floor (2 years CI/CD, 2 years databases, 1 year Hadoop/Spark, 1 year data hygiene) is comfortably cleared. Meets/exceeds → 5. The Summary's '6 years of experience in Data engineering' self-claim is excluded and is not needed; the dated segments carry more than it claims.

education_domain_fit.notes: Master of Science in Business Analytics (University of Texas at Dallas, 2020) — the only education entry; no bachelor's is listed, though a master's presupposes one. The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level technical education for a data engineering role. Level is met and exceeded by the master's. Field is business analytics — data-oriented and therefore related, but not core to data engineering, which is a software engineering discipline; business analytics trains the interpretation of data rather than the building of pipelines. Level met + field related-rather-than-core → 3, not sub-graded. Consistent with the reading applied to train 596's Computer Applications and train 1050's Management Information Systems.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's 'Should have' and year-floor requirements. Met: pipeline operationalization, Python/PySpark (both the skill and its 4-year floor), CI/CD tooling, Hadoop/Spark, and data hygiene — each reusing a skills_coverage determination backed by hands-on duty evidence. Three indeterminate, none unmet. Azure Databricks/Spark/Azure Data Factory = indeterminate: the determination is covered, but covered by adjacency alone on a vendor-specific must in a role titled Azure Data Engineer — AWS EMR is not Azure Databricks, and whether a hiring manager accepts the transfer is exactly the kind of call a reasonable annotator could make either way. Software engineering fundamentals = indeterminate (adjacent evidence only; algorithm theory and data structures never named). Relational and NoSQL databases = indeterminate: the relational half is strong and the NoSQL half is absent, which is a partial rather than a majority-absent bundle, so the unmet rule does not reach it and 'cannot decide' is the honest record. No item clearly unmet, three indeterminate → 3; the soft veto fires in its indeterminate mode → force_gate only, no aggregate cap. The second pair in the set to reach the indeterminate branch, and the one where it fits best — a human is being asked to rule on cloud-vendor transferability, which is precisely a judgment a screener should own.

hesitations: The rubric and the dataset agree here, and the agreement is worth recording after so much divergence: dataset says Potential Fit; the rubric returns 3.1 with an indeterminate veto and a forced gate, which is the same verdict in different vocabulary. boundary recorded because the advance/reject call genuinely could go either way — this is a capable data engineer with 8.5 years, deep Spark/Hadoop/Python and real pipeline work, whose gaps are (a) the wrong cloud vendor and (b) no NoSQL. Both gaps are plausibly closeable by a competent engineer, and a screener might reasonably advance or reject on either reading. Recorded judgment: the Azure adjacency call is the single most consequential in this label. Reading AWS EMR as adjacent to Azure Databricks keeps the item covered and the band at 2; reading it as absent — on the ground that the JD says Azure three times and means it — would make the bundle majority-absent and drop the band to 1, the mean to 2.6, and the veto to unmet. The adjacency reading was taken because the rubric's own example (PostgreSQL for MySQL) is precisely a same-capability, different-vendor substitution, and refusing it here would apply a stricter standard than the rubric states. The owner may want to rule on whether vendor-specific musts resist adjacency, since cloud-vendor mismatch will recur across this corpus.

skills_coverage.notes: Every skills requirement in this JD is absent, and the gap is categorical rather than marginal: the posting wants an Ansible developer and the resume is a career sales executive. Ansible, AWX and Tower — which the JD marks in capitals as a hard requirement and MUST HAVE — appear nowhere, nor do playbooks. Neither do shell scripting, Oracle queries, REST APIs, Python, Git, CI/CD, Kubernetes or OpenShift. Agile methodologies are absent too. Nothing is adjacent: the candidate sold software for a living — dynaTrace, Shunra, Tivoli, Network Physics, all technical products — and one VP role covered 'product spec, design and development to testing', but selling, managing and going to market around engineering is not transferable evidence of doing it. All determinations absent → band 0, the all-absent case. Bundling note: the JD's skills are a single unpunctuated run-on ('... Shell scripting Oracle Database queries understanding Reverse engineer programming languages Agile methodologies REST API gitcode reviewtesting CICD Python K8sOpenshift'), so where one requirement ends and the next begins is genuinely ambiguous. They are grouped here into four items along the JD's own line breaks. The grouping is arbitrary and it is recorded as such, but it cannot affect the outcome — every component is absent under any grouping, so the band is 0 whether this is four bundles or ten separate musts.

determinations.requirement: Ansible / AWX / Tower, and Ansible playbooks (marked hard requirement, MUST HAVE)
Shell scripting; Oracle Database queries understanding
Reverse engineer programming languages; Agile methodologies; REST API
Git / code review / testing; CI/CD; Python; K8s / OpenShift

experience_level.notes: The JD states no year count, so proximity is judged against the role: a software developer writing Ansible playbooks in a DevOps pipeline. The resume's dated history is long and well-formed — Tivoli/IBM 1996–2000, NativeMinds 2000–2002, Network Physics 2003–2007, dynaTrace 2008–2010, Shunra 2010–2011, HP VP Strategic Sales 09/2011–11/2012, HP VP Information Mgmt SaaS 09/2012–2015, HPE VP Business Development 01/2015–current — roughly 28 years, and every segment is sales, channel or business-development leadership. Zero role-matching segments for a developer role; no segment contains a single engineering duty. Substantial, senior, entirely off-occupation experience → band 1, the same shape as train 596, 400 and 1089.

education_domain_fit.notes: BA in Sociology (Bates College) and MA in International Economics & US Foreign Policy (Johns Hopkins, Nitze School of Advanced International Studies). The JD asks for no degree, so the ask is role-implied: technical bachelor's-level education for a software developer position. Level is met twice over — this is a well-credentialed candidate. Field is the disqualifier: sociology and international economics are unrelated to software development, and neither is adjacent by any reading. Field unrelated → 1, following the same reading as train 3559's BA in Art. Level fitting while field is unrelated again sits between band 1's 'Neither fits' headline and its disjunctive criteria text; the criteria text governs, as it has throughout this batch.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's skills list, all of which it presents as requirements. All four skills items = unmet, reusing the skills_coverage determinations — including the Ansible item the JD marks twice over as a hard requirement and MUST HAVE, the most emphatic requirement marking anywhere in this sample. W2-only engagement (no Corp to Corp) = indeterminate: employment-type and engagement constraints sit alongside visas and location in the rubric's ledger categories, the JD states it as a position condition, and the resume says nothing about how the candidate would contract — cannot decide, so recorded rather than assumed, consistent with the visa handling in train 901 and the relocation handling in train 1050. Four clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate).

hesitations: The most extreme owner-vs-dataset disagreement in the set: the dataset labels this Potential Fit, and the rubric returns 0.5 — its lowest aggregate so far — with an unmet veto on an explicitly-flagged MUST HAVE. A VP of Sales with a BA in Sociology and an MA in International Economics is not a potential fit for an Ansible developer contract; there is no reading of this posting's requirements under which he is. Where earlier disagreements (train 5063, train 3229) were explicable as the dataset tracking candidate calibre rather than requirement coverage, that explanation does not survive here — this candidate is senior and accomplished, but in a different profession entirely, so even a seniority-tracking label should not reach Potential Fit. This looks like a mislabeled row, and it is the strongest evidence yet that the dataset's three classes cannot be used as a base layer without review. Recommend the owner inspect this pair alongside train 970 (the mirror case: a strong, well-matched data analyst labeled No Fit). Corpus artifact, noted but deliberately NOT flagged as an anomaly, consistent with the line drawn in train 2980: the employer fields are evidently substituted — the current role is titled 'VP, Business Development, Information Mgmt & Governance BU, HPE Software' while its employer field reads 'Co-Op Financial Services–Jpl,TX', and the two 2011–2015 HP roles carry 'City,STATE' placeholders — and the trailing 'Skills' section is empty, its content having apparently been absorbed into a 'Skill Highlights' block that actually contains a second copy of the work history. This substitution pattern recurs across the sample (train 970's 'Alteryx Inc.', train 3229's 'Sofi', train 2980's 'Jacobs Engineering Group Inc.–Virginia Beach,,India') and belongs in the P0 report as a corpus-wide note rather than a per-pair flag; nothing here is unparseable, so the anomaly reason is withheld.

skills_coverage.notes: Ten stated skills musts, and the JD's whole stack is Java — Java web development, Spring Boot/MVC, Spring Batch, JPA/Hibernate, Angular SPA, REST, microservices, JUnit. The resume contains no Java at all. It is an OBIEE/BI developer's document: Oracle Business Intelligence Enterprise Edition, BI Publisher, Informatica DAC, RPD and dashboard development, SQL and Oracle 9.x/10g. Eight of the ten are absent outright with nothing adjacent — SQL against Oracle is not adjacent to JPA/Hibernate object-relational mapping, and OBIEE report building is not adjacent to Angular single-page applications. Two are partial rather than absent, and both for the same reason: the JD pairs a generic practice the candidate does have with specific tooling he does not. Agile/Scrum — the collaborative development team environment is evidenced in duties (peer code reviews, unit testing, SIT, coordinating across SDLC phases with ETL and QA teams), but Agile and Scrum are never named, and only SDLC is. Unit testing — 'Design, Build, Unit Testing OBI Reports' evidences the practice, but JUnit, JMockit and Mockito are all absent, and they are Java frameworks the candidate has no route to. Eight absent → band 1. Duplicate merged: the JD states the JPA/Hibernate data-access requirement twice ('dealing with the Data Access layer using JPA and Hibernate' and 'Hands-on data access experience using JPA or spring data JPA with hibernate') and the Agile/Scrum team requirement twice verbatim; each is recorded once rather than double-listed, following the handling in train 901.
