Collaborative development team environment using Agile/Scrum methodologies
Data access layer using JPA and Hibernate (incl. spring data JPA) with caching-based performance tuning
Solid Java Development using latest features (Streams, Lambda, Predicates)
Single Page Application web development using Angular 2+, Bootstrap/Material, HTML5, CSS3, NodeJS
Working knowledge of REST services
Development experience in Spring Boot / Spring MVC framework
Knowledge of Microservices
Writing Spring batch jobs
Unit tests for components and services using JUnit and/or JMockit/Mockito

experience_level.notes: The JD's years ask is specific: work experience in JAVA DEVELOPMENT — 2 years against a master's, 4 against a bachelor's. Dated segments are well-formed: Assistant Systems Engineer at TCS 11/2008–12/2010 (~2.1 yrs) and Senior Software Engineer 01/2011–05/2013 (~2.3 yrs), about 4.4 years total. The titles are software engineering and would tempt a match, but the duties in both segments are entirely OBIEE — repository and dashboard fixes, RPD development, OBIEE upgrade assessment, report performance tuning, triage with ETL and QA teams. Java appears in neither segment, so under the role-matching rule (a skill's years accrue only from segments whose role or duties match that skill) exactly zero Java years are evidenced — this is the rubric's own 'Java years accrue in a Java Developer role' example, in the negative. No role-matching dated segments for the stated skill → band 1. The Summary's 'more than 5 years as a software engineer' is an undated self-claim, excluded, and it claims software engineering generally rather than Java. Recorded tension: 4.4 years of real software engineering scoring band 1 looks harsh next to the band-3 pairs in this batch, but the difference is principled — those had role-matching duties and fell short on quantity, whereas this has no role-matching duties for the skill asked at all.

education_domain_fit.notes: Master of Computer Applications with the field recorded as Computer Science (R.V.R & J.C College of Engineering, 2008), plus a BSc in Computers/Mathematics (2005). The JD asks a master's in Computer Science, MIS or related field — met exactly, at the higher of the two levels its disjunction offers. Field is computer science, squarely within the software development occupation → 5. Reading note for consistency with train 596, which scored a 'Bachelor of Science:Applied Computing' at 3: these documents record degree and field in separate slots, and the field slot governs. There the field slot read 'Applied Computing', an applications/IT orientation that is related-rather-than-core; here it reads 'Computer Science', which is core. Same rule, different field values, different bands. This is also the fourth pair in the batch where a top-band education sits beside bottom-band skills and experience — well-credentialed for the profession, no evidence of this particular job.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the Qualifications block. Degree = met (the compound qualification's degree half is satisfied outright — a master's in computer science clears both branches of the JD's disjunction). Java Development years = unmet: the same qualification's experience half fails in both branches, since zero Java years are evidenced against either the 2-year or the 4-year floor, reusing the experience_level determination. The compound is decomposed into its two halves rather than ledgered as one item, so the met degree is recorded honestly and does not obscure the unmet experience — but note the JD joins them with AND inside each disjunct, so satisfying the degree alone qualifies nobody. Unmet, each reusing a skills_coverage determination: Java web frameworks, JPA/Hibernate data access, solid Java, Angular SPA, REST services, Spring Boot/MVC, microservices, and Spring batch jobs. Indeterminate: Agile/Scrum (collaborative team practice evidenced, the named methodologies not) and JUnit/Mockito unit testing (the practice evidenced, the Java frameworks not) — both partials a reasonable annotator could judge either way. Nine clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate).

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement, mild in size but clear in direction: the dataset says Potential Fit, the rubric returns 1.8 with an unmet veto. The rubric's reading looks right on stated requirements — this is a competent BI developer with a computer science master's and 4.4 years of software engineering applying to a Java role that names Java or a Java framework in nine of its ten requirements, and he has none of them. What makes 'Potential Fit' comprehensible is the adjacency of profession rather than of skill: he is a software engineer, and could plausibly learn Java. The rubric deliberately does not score potential, only evidence, and the split between the two is exactly what this pair records. Consistent with train 5063, 3229 and 3861, the dataset's classes continue to look like a judgment about the candidate rather than about the JD's musts. Recorded judgment: the Agile/Scrum and unit-testing items were called partial rather than absent, and the choice matters less than it appears — the band is pinned at 1 by eight absent determinations either way, and the veto is already unmet on the Java years. The reading is stated for consistency with the head-skill/exemplar treatment used across this batch: where a JD pairs a practice with named tooling, evidence of the practice alone makes the requirement partial, not covered and not absent.

skills_coverage.notes: This JD puts almost all its substance under 'Preferred Skills' — the eligibility/enrollment/claims domain knowledge, mainframe modernization requirements, As-Is/To-Be process analysis, use cases and screen prototypes, Blueprint Storyteller, meeting facilitation — and stance A excludes every one of them from the band. What remains is a single skills must, and it arrives embedded in a years clause: client-focused business and requirements analysis, including leading requirements gathering sessions and preparing technology proposals. Per the boundary rule the quantity half is scored under experience_level and only the skill's presence is judged here. It is partial. The head skill is genuinely evidenced and hands-on: needs assessments conducted to define user requirements, business requirements analyzed and automated against, reporting requirements established across an organization, specs and test cases documented for each PL/SQL procedure — and the client-focused qualifier holds too, since the work is client data, client systems and client-facing support throughout. But both named components are absent: nothing evidences LEADING requirements gathering sessions (no facilitation, no workshops, no workgroups) and nothing evidences preparing technology proposals from elicited requirements. Some components present, some absent → partial → caps the band at 2. Derived-musts does not apply — the JD states this skills must, so the rule's zero-stated-musts trigger is not reached, even though nearly all of this posting's skill content sits in a preferred block the rubric discards.

determinations.requirement: Client-focused business and requirements analysis, including leading requirements gathering sessions and preparing technology proposals from elicited requirements

experience_level.notes: Two stacked 7-year floors plus a 2-year Business Analyst leadership floor. Dated segments are well-formed: Programmer Analyst 03/2012–06/2015 (~3.3 yrs), HR Intern 07/2016–03/2017 (~0.7 yr), Data Analyst 08/2017–01/2020 (~2.4 yrs), Data Analyst 08/2020–01/2022 (~1.4 yrs) — about 7.8 years total. Role-matching against requirements analysis is partly satisfied: the Programmer Analyst and the 2017–2020 Data Analyst segments both carry requirements duties (analyzing business requirements, needs assessment to define user requirements, establishing structures to meet reporting requirements), which is roughly 5.7 years; the 2020–2022 segment is data reconciliation and quality work with little requirements content, and the HR internship does not accrue at all. So the raw total brushes the 7-year floor while the role-matching total falls short of it. The seniority gap is cleaner and decides the band: the JD wants a Level III BA with two years leading as a Business Analyst, and the candidate has never held a Business Analyst title or any leadership role — every segment is an individual-contributor analyst post. Clear relevant experience in role-matching segments, with a real gap in both years and seniority → 3.

education_domain_fit.notes: Bachelor of Engineering in Computer Science and Engineering (Anna University, 2011), MBA in Human Resources and Marketing Management (Bharathiar, 2017), and a Master of Science in Data Science (University of Memphis, 2024). The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level education for a senior technical Business Analyst doing system requirements analysis and mainframe modernization. Level is met several times over. Field fits squarely on both halves of what this occupation actually is — the computer science degree covers the system-requirements and technology side, and the MBA covers the business-analysis side; a technical BA sits exactly at that intersection → 5. Consistency note against train 935, where an Electrical Engineering degree drew a 3 for a technical BA role: computer science is core to system requirements analysis where electrical engineering is merely related, and the MBA here adds business-domain education that pair had none of.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the Basic Qualifications block — the JD's only non-preferred requirements. 7 years on complex projects with 2 years in a Business Analyst leadership role = unmet: the leadership half has no evidence at all, no Business Analyst title and no lead role in any of the four dated segments, so this fails clearly rather than ambiguously. 7 years of client-focused business and requirements analysis = indeterminate: about 7.8 years are dated and requirements-analysis duties genuinely recur, but they are components of Data Analyst and Programmer Analyst roles rather than the roles themselves, and the role-matching subtotal falls under the floor — whether that clears 'client-focused business and requirements analysis experience' is a call a reasonable annotator could make either way. The requirements-analysis skill = indeterminate (partial: the analysis is evidenced, leading sessions and preparing proposals are not). One clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). The two indeterminates change nothing — the ledger has no ratio.

hesitations: The rubric and dataset land in broadly the same place for once — dataset Potential Fit, rubric 2.9 with a forced gate — and the pair is a fair example of what the veto is for: a candidate with real, relevant analytical experience who is missing a specific, checkable, stated requirement (two years leading as a BA). Recorded observation about this JD's structure rather than about the candidate: stance A does very heavy lifting here. Every concrete skill this posting describes — eligibility/enrollment/case management/claims domain knowledge, mainframe modernization, As-Is/To-Be documentation, use cases and screen prototypes, Blueprint Storyteller, facilitating workgroups — sits in the Preferred Skills block and is therefore excluded, even though the Duties section restates most of it as the actual job. The result is that a 3,620-character JD contributes exactly one requirement to the 0.5-weight dimension. This is a milder cousin of the one-thin-must problem recorded at train 935: there the JD was terse, here it is verbose but files its substance under the wrong heading. Both suggest the same rubric-revision question for the owner — whether derived-musts (or some equivalent) should reach JDs whose stated skills musts are vestigial, not merely absent. Noting also that the Duties section here would have supplied rich derived requirements had the rule been allowed to run, and that doing so would likely have lowered this band rather than raised it, since the domain-specific items are all absent from the resume.

skills_coverage.notes: Four stated skills musts, three of them marked '(required)' in the JD's own parentheses. Excel is covered with hands-on evidence — not merely listed but used to build a new financial recording system — and in-house payroll processing is likewise hands-on (payroll processed along with electronic deposits and pay adjustments). QuickBooks is the decisive absence: it appears nowhere in the resume, and nothing is adjacent to it. Excel is not an adjacent substitute for an accounting package, and the rubric's adjacency test is transferable capability, not neighbouring category; the JD is specific about the tool because the job is processing payroll through QuickBooks Desktop. The absence is also informative rather than incidental — this is an accountant's resume that does name its tooling (Excel), so QuickBooks would appear if it were there. Typing at 40 WPM is absent on the evidence: no typing speed is stated anywhere, and there is nothing adjacent to a WPM figure. One absent determination is enough → band 1 by the geometry, notwithstanding two hands-on covered items. Excluded as dispositional boilerplate, per the stance applied across this batch: strong communication skills, attention to detail, initiative and a solutions-oriented mindset, professional and friendly demeanor, flexibility in a fast-paced environment, and maintaining confidentiality.

determinations.requirement: Expertise with MS Excel (required)
Expertise with QuickBooks (required)
In-house payroll processing (required)
Typing skills at a minimum of 40 WPM

experience_level.notes: The JD states no years floor at all — no minimum, no seniority level beyond the role title — so proximity is judged against the clearly implied ask: an Office Manager/Accountant handling bookkeeping, payroll, reconciliations, month and year-end financial reports and CPA hand-off. The single dated segment, Accountant 06/2012–09/2015 (~3.3 yrs), is role-matching on duties in unusual detail: accounts receivable documentation, bank reconciliations, weekly and monthly financial reports, payroll processing and pay adjustments, and collaboration with auditors through preliminary and year-end audit — nearly a line-for-line match with the JD's responsibilities. Evidenced relevant experience meets the implied requirement in a role-matching segment → 5. Recorded observation the band does not capture: the work history ends 09/2015 and the resume offers nothing after it, which against a 2024-era posting is a long unexplained gap. The rubric scores evidenced relevant years and seniority, not recency or continuity, so the gap does not move this dimension — but it is the kind of thing a human screener would ask about, and it is invisible in this label.

education_domain_fit.notes: BA (Honours) in Business Studies, Second Upper Class (University of Greenwich, 2014), plus Association of Business Executive diplomas at Levels 3, 5 and 6 whose units include Financial Accounting, Managerial Accounting and Corporate Finance. The JD asks for no degree, so the ask is role-implied: bookkeeping/accounting education for an Office Manager/Accountant. Level is met by the bachelor's. Field is business studies — related to the accounting occupation and clearly accounting-adjacent, but not accounting itself; the degree's own listed units are strategy, international business management and small business development rather than accounting, and the accounting content sits in the ABE diplomas, which are sub-degree awards and so do not carry this dimension. Level met + field related-rather-than-core → 3, not sub-graded. Consistency note against train 3559, where a BA in Art drew a 1 for an accounting JD: business studies is genuinely adjacent to accounting where art is unrelated, and the distinction is exactly the one band 3 versus band 1 is drawing.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the Selection Criteria. MS Excel = met and in-house payroll processing = met, both reusing hands-on skills_coverage determinations. Business correspondence, filing and organization = met. QuickBooks = unmet: explicitly marked '(required)', absent from the document, and absent informatively — the resume names Excel, so it names its tools. Typing at 40 WPM = INDETERMINATE despite its absent determination, and the divergence is deliberate. skills_coverage asks whether a skill is evidenced, and it is not; the ledger asks whether the requirement is satisfied, which is a different question, and a resume's silence on typing speed is not evidence that the candidate types below 40 WPM — essentially no accountant states a WPM figure, so absence here carries no signal. That is 'cannot decide', which is what band 3 is reserved for. The contrast with QuickBooks is the point: an absent skill that the document type would normally report (accounting software) supports unmet; an absent skill that no document of this type ever reports (typing speed) supports only indeterminate. One clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate).

hesitations: Direction agrees with the dataset's Potential Fit — rubric returns 2.6 with a forced gate — and the pair is a clean illustration of the veto doing useful work: an otherwise well-matched accountant, whose dated duties mirror the JD almost line for line, is gated on one specific, checkable, stated tool. That is exactly the call a human should own, and QuickBooks is learnable in a week, which is precisely why the rubric caps and gates rather than auto-rejecting. anomaly recorded for an unfilled template field: the education block's first entry reads 'Select  One:,Expected in--,GPA:Status-' with no degree, no institution and no date — the same 'Select...' corruption seen in train 1089. Corpus artifact noted but deliberately not part of the anomaly call, consistent with train 2980 and train 3861: the employer fields are substituted, and here the substitution produces a flat contradiction inside a single entry — the header reads 'Accountant,06/2012-09/2015Ametek, Inc.–Wilmington,MA' while the entry's own first sentence says 'I have worked at Shantilal Gala & Company (Nairobi, Kenya) which is an Audit firm, since 2nd July 2012'. Unlike the metadata-only substitutions elsewhere, this one is visible in the prose a reader would cite, so an agent asked to name the employer for this segment has two contradictory answers and no way to choose. Worth carrying into the P0 report with the other substitution examples.

skills_coverage.notes: Seven skills musts, each stated as a year-floor; per the boundary rule the quantities are scored under experience_level and only skill presence and strength are judged here. Five are covered and four of those hands-on, which makes this a much stronger match than the band suggests: requirements elicitation and business process review are evidenced in detail (prioritizing user requirements, creating Purchasing RFPs, scrubbing data to conversion requirements, workflow diagrams); facilitation to consensus is explicit ('Duties included consensus building', leading users through proposal scoring to a decision); contract compliance is named verbatim as a duty and backed by collaborating with Purchasing and Law on a contract; and regulated-programs work is strong — HIPAA Compliance Officer, HIPAA training, auditing and investigating HIPAA complaints, which is federal regulation squarely. Strategic analysis is covered by adjacency only: policy development, vetting software solutions against changing needs and running RFP-to-implementation programs are adjacent to 'strategic analysis and support to program, division and agency initiatives', but the JD's public-sector framing has no direct counterpart in a clinic setting. Two items are absent and they pin the band: Agile Scrum, JIRA, user stories, sprint planning and backlog grooming — the entire bundle, no keyword and nothing adjacent — and LEAN principles with LPI initiatives, likewise entirely absent. Two absent → band 1. Recorded tension: a band of 1 on a document that covers five of seven requirements, four hands-on, reads harsher than the evidence feels. The geometry is doing what it was designed to do — absent pins the band regardless of the rest, and the rubric explicitly refuses a ratio — but this pair is the clearest case in the batch of the whole-dimension rule compressing a genuinely partial match into the bottom band.

determinations.requirement: Business process review, requirements elicitation and documentation (min 7 years)
Facilitating executive and diverse groups to topic consensus (min 5 years)
Strategic analysis and support to program, division and agency initiatives (min 5 years)
Agile Scrum, JIRA, user story development/validation, sprint planning, backlog grooming (min 3 years)
Contract compliance and contract change notices specific to requirements specifications (min 2 years)
Practicing LEAN principles and supporting LPI initiatives (min 2 years)
Working with programs governed by state and/or federal regulations (min 1 year)

experience_level.notes: The JD stacks eight year-floors, from 12 years of general BA experience down to 1 year of regulated-programs work. The resume offers a single dated segment — Health Data Analyst 09/2001–07/2019, about 17.8 years — and it is role-matching on duties rather than title: the segment's own duties are requirements gathering, RFP authoring and scoring, consensus building, contract negotiation and compliance, system implementation and training. That is business analysis in substance, so under the role-matching rule the years accrue, and the headline 12-year floor is cleared comfortably, as are the 7-year, 5-year facilitation, 2-year contract and 1-year regulated floors, all evidenced inside that same span. Two floors sit at zero: 3 years of Agile/JIRA and 2 years of LEAN, neither of which appears anywhere in an 18-year career. Clear relevant experience in a role-matching segment, with real gaps against two stated floors → 3. Note this is a years gap, not merely a skills gap: the JD asks for a quantity of Agile and LEAN practice and the evidenced quantity is zero, which is what keeps this off band 5 rather than the skills determinations doing it twice.

education_domain_fit.notes: MBA in Business Administration (Alabama A&M, summa cum laude) plus a BS in Banking/Finance (Missouri State). The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level business education for a Business Analyst post. Level is met and exceeded. Field fits squarely — this is a business analyst role in substance (business process review, requirements elicitation, facilitation to consensus, contract compliance, LEAN), not a systems or software analyst role, and business administration is core to it rather than adjacent; the finance bachelor's reinforces the same discipline. Both halves fit → 5. Consistency note against train 935, where an Electrical Engineering degree drew a 3: that JD was an explicitly technical BA role capturing 'functional, and technical requirements' and documenting system interactions, so an engineering degree was related-rather-than-core; here the occupation's centre of gravity is business process, and a business degree is core to it. The JD-relative rule is what produces the different bands, and it is working as intended.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's eight stated minimums. Met: 12 years general BA, 7 years business process review and requirements elicitation, 5 years facilitation to consensus, 2 years contract compliance, and 1 year of regulated-programs work — each reusing a skills_coverage or experience_level determination, and each evidenced inside the single 17.8-year role-matching segment. Unmet: 3 years Agile Scrum/JIRA/user stories/sprint planning (bundle absent) and 2 years LEAN/LPI (absent) — both clearly unmet rather than ambiguous, since an 18-year work history that never mentions either supports a confident negative. Indeterminate: 5 years strategic analysis at program/division/agency level (adjacent evidence only — policy development and software-selection programs in a clinic are not obviously the public-sector strategic support asked for, and a reasonable annotator could go either way), and the Michigan-local requirement (the JD marks it emphatically, location sits in the rubric's ledger categories, and the resume states no candidate location at all — only employer and university locations across Virginia, Alabama and Missouri, which are not the candidate's address; cannot decide, so recorded rather than inferred, consistent with train 1050 and train 3861). Two clear unmet → 0 — five met items buy nothing, the ledger has no ratio; soft veto fires (cap + gate).

hesitations: Direction agrees with the dataset's Potential Fit; rubric returns 2.4 with a forced gate. boundary recorded because this is a genuine either-way call: an 18-year analyst who covers five of the seven stated requirement areas — several of them hands-on and one, HIPAA compliance, at officer level — and misses two, Agile/JIRA and LEAN, both of which are learnable methodologies rather than deep capabilities. A screener could defensibly advance her to interview or reject on the missing minimums, and that is exactly the decision the rubric routes to a human. Recorded observation about this JD rather than this candidate: it is a recruiter's email pasted whole, complete with greeting, the recruiter's name, direct phone number and email address, and it files all eight of its requirements under a header reading 'Responsibilities:'. So the JD's structural markers are unreliable — the requirements are not where the labels say they are, and a screening agent keying on section headers would find no requirements section at all. No anomaly flag: nothing here is corrupt or unparseable, and the requirements are legible to a reader, which is the line held throughout this batch. Worth noting for the P0 report that JD-side quality in this corpus varies as much as resume-side quality, and that recruiter-email postings carry third-party PII.

skills_coverage.notes: This is a Salesforce role — the title says so, and Salesforce, SFDC, Vlocity, Omnistudio or Salesforce.com appear in six of the JD's requirement lines — and the resume contains no Salesforce content whatsoever. Six determinations are absent: the Communications cloud, the SFDC platform and its project lifecycle, the Vlocity/Omnistudio/API architecture bundle, owning architecture design and solutioning, and reviewing and documenting code. Nothing is adjacent to any of them. The candidate is a Software Quality Assurance leader and project manager; leading the implementation of software solutions is not architecting them, and conducting traceability reviews against test cases is not reviewing code. Two items are partial rather than absent. Telecom domain solutioning for Quote to Cash: the telecom half is real but thin — 'PBX Telecom' in the qualifications list, 'Telecommunications' in the skills blob, a 5ESS MTCE training course, and a Career Overview claiming focus on telecommunications industries — while Quote to Cash, and solutioning generally, are absent. Source control and deployments: maintaining multiple QA environments across multiple releases is adjacent to deployment-to-higher-environments, but source control itself is absent. Six absent → band 1. Excluded by stance A: 'Certification a plus'.

Against the SFDC floor the role-matching total is zero: neither segment contains a single Salesforce duty, so none of those 10 years accrues to the skill the JD actually asks for, and the numerical coincidence between 10.2 years of career and the 10-year SFDC floor is exactly the trap the role-matching rule exists to close. Against the Telecom floor it is nearly as bare: CME is a financial exchange and Flowserve is industrial, so no dated segment is telecom work — the telecom association lives only in the undated Career Overview self-claim and a PBX keyword, both excluded or insufficient. education_domain_fit.notes: BS in Business/eBusiness (University of Phoenix distance program, 2006). The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level education for a Salesforce solution architect/BA. Level is met. Field is business with an eBusiness concentration — related to a CRM-platform business analyst role, and the eBusiness element gives it a technology orientation, but this JD's centre of gravity is technical (architecting on Vlocity, Salesforce API frameworks, third-party APIs, source control, code review), and a business degree is not core to that. Level met + field related-rather-than-core → 3, not sub-graded. The training list (5ESS MTCE, Cold Fusion, QA and PM courses) is non-degree and excluded from this dimension, though the Cold Fusion and telecom courses are the closest thing here to technical education.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the Business Requirements block. Unmet, each reusing a skills_coverage determination: Salesforce Industries Communications cloud, SFDC platform and project lifecycle, the Vlocity/Omnistudio/API architecture bundle, owning architecture design and solutioning, and reviewing and documenting code. Also unmet: the 10+ years SFDC floor, reusing the experience_level determination that zero SFDC years are evidenced. Indeterminate: Telecom/Quote-to-Cash solutioning (partial — telecom keywords and training exist, Quote to Cash does not); source control and deployments (partial — release and environment management is adjacent, source control is absent); leading solution discussions with customer IT and business (consulting end users on requirements and reporting to senior management is adjacent, but presenting solution architecture options is a different act); and guiding onsite-offshore development teams (the candidate demonstrably managed onsite and offshore teams, but as QA staff rather than developers translating architecture into implementation — genuinely judgeable either way). Six clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate).

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement: the dataset says Potential Fit; the rubric returns 1.4 with an unmet veto. On stated requirements the rubric looks right and the gap is not close — this is a Salesforce Communications Cloud architect role asking 10+ years of SFDC and hands-on Vlocity/Omnistudio, and the candidate has never touched Salesforce. What likely drives the dataset's label is surface overlap that the rubric correctly refuses to credit: the resume mentions telecommunications, mentions requirements gathering, mentions onsite-offshore teams, and shows ~10 years of senior experience against a 10-year floor — every one of which is a keyword coincidence rather than evidence of the asked-for skill. That makes this pair a useful adversarial case for the agent evaluation: an agent doing shallow matching would likely score it far higher than the requirements justify, and the rubric's role-matching rule is precisely what separates the two readings. Consistent with train 5063, 3229, 3861 and 3978, the dataset's classes continue to look like a judgment about candidate seniority and topical adjacency rather than about the JD's musts. No anomaly flag — this document is well-formed and its dates are intact; the employer fields show the usual substitution pattern (a QA manager for a financial exchange and an industrial pump manufacturer, described in a Career Overview about financial and telecommunications industries), which is noted corpus-wide rather than per pair.

skills_coverage.notes: Four stated skills musts, all covered, and the band turns on evidence strength rather than presence — the first time in this batch. Three are hands-on and substantial: building web software (monolith decoupled to microservices on Node.js and RESTful APIs, a Market microservice built on PostgreSQL/Express/TypeScript), building and scaling products (an ingestion pipeline serving millions of daily entries, IoT alerting prototypes), and architecting and deploying to a cloud provider (a GCP pipeline designed and deployed end to end with Pub/Sub, Dataflow, BigQuery and Stackdriver — the JD names Google Cloud first among its three acceptable providers). The essential skill is the weak one. Java and Spring Boot are both present, but only in the skills list — 'Python, Java, HTML, CSS, JavaScript ... Spring, Spring MVC, Spring Boot, Spring Data, Spring Cloud' — and neither appears in a single work-history duty; every technology actually exercised in the duties is TypeScript, JavaScript, Node.js, Python, Express, BigQuery or Apache Beam. So the JD's one essential skill, asked for as 'deep experience', is traceable at keyword level and nowhere evidenced in use. All covered with no partial and no absent; majority hands-on with a keyword minority → band 4 by the geometry (the interpolation band). Excluded by stance A: React ('a plus') and the entire Helpful skills block (AWS, Kubernetes, Docker, Argo CD, Atlas, GitHub Actions) — worth noting that the candidate does have k8s and Docker listed, so stance A costs him credit here rather than sparing him.

Evidenced years exceed the floor in role-matching segments → 5. Notable and recorded: the Professional Summary claims 'more than 4 years', which is LESS than the ~8.2 years the dated segments evidence — the self-claim would have understated the candidate. The rubric excludes Summary self-claims on principle, and here that exclusion helps rather than hurts him; it is a useful counterexample to the assumption that self-claims always inflate.

education_domain_fit.notes: BS in Electrical, Electronics Engineering Technologies (Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology, 2011) and a Master of Science from Syracuse University (2014) whose FIELD IS BLANK — the entry reads 'Master of Science:,' with nothing between the colon and the comma, so the document states the level but not the discipline. The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level technical education for a senior full-stack engineer. Level is met by the bachelor's alone and exceeded by the master's whatever its field. Field is scored on the bachelor's, the only one the document identifies: electrical and electronics engineering technology is technical and related to software engineering, but not core to it — the same reading given to train 400's ECET degree. Level met + field related-rather-than-core → 3, not sub-graded. The missing master's field is recorded rather than guessed: it could plausibly be computer science, which would lift this to a 5, but the rubric scores evidenced content and a blank field evidences nothing.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's Qualifications and 'We're looking for someone who' blocks. Met: web/mobile software building, building and scaling products, cloud architecture and deployment, and the 5+ year full-stack floor — each reusing a hands-on skills_coverage or experience_level determination. Java + Spring Boot = INDETERMINATE, and it is the whole ledger here. The determination is covered, so the unmet rule does not reach it — both components are present at keyword level and neither is absent. But the JD does not merely ask for Java and Spring Boot, it asks for DEEP experience with them, names them its only essential skills, and the resume evidences them nowhere but a keyword list while showing a different stack in every actual duty. Whether a skills-list mention satisfies 'deep experience' is exactly the kind of call a reasonable annotator could make either way, which is band 3's reservation — and it is the difference between a strong hire and a mismatch, so it is the right question to route to a human. No item clearly unmet, one indeterminate → 3; the soft veto fires in its indeterminate mode → force_gate only, no aggregate cap.

hesitations: Direction agrees with the dataset's Potential Fit: rubric returns 4.1 — the second-highest aggregate in the set — with an indeterminate veto forcing a gate, which is a fair rendering of 'strong, but check one thing'. boundary recorded because the advance/reject call rests entirely on the Java/Spring Boot question: this is a capable senior engineer with 8+ years, real cloud architecture and real microservice work, whose fit for a Java/Spring Boot role depends on whether the two keywords in his skills list reflect actual depth or aspiration. A recruiter would resolve that in one phone call, which is precisely why the rubric gates rather than rejects. Recorded observation, and it is the sharpest instance in the batch of a tension noted at train 400: band 3 explicitly accepts skills-list mentions, so a keyword blob can carry a requirement the work history contradicts. Here it does not merely reach the floor, it helps produce a band 4 — because the geometry counts covered items and the three hands-on items outvote the one keyword item, the dimension scores 4 while the JD's single essential skill remains unevidenced in use. The weighting is arguably inverted: the JD says Java/Spring Boot is essential and the rest is context, but the geometry treats all four determinations as equals. Rubric-revision material for the owner — whether a JD's explicitly 'essential' skill should carry more weight within the dimension than co-equal treatment gives it, or whether keyword-only evidence on an essential skill should cap the band. Recording rather than overriding, per the standing stance.

skills_coverage.notes: Five stated skills musts under a header that could not be plainer — 'All candidates must meet the qualifications below' — and four are covered, three of them hands-on and deep. Data marts and data modeling: logical and physical data models developed, entities and cardinality identified, DataMart design across a 70-mart Netezza migration. SQL: the head skill is hands-on throughout (database code and stored procedures produced to spec, Netezza SQL issues resolved, PL/SQL mapplets written), and while the parenthetical's three dialects — MySQL, Postgres, Redshift — are all absent, the parenthetical exemplifies which SQL is meant rather than listing coordinate requirements, and Oracle, SQL Server, DB2, Netezza and Snowflake are clearly adjacent dialects. ETL: the JD says 'any ETL tools', and DataStage, Informatica PowerCentre and SSIS are hands-on across every dated segment; Airflow and Boomi being absent does not matter when the requirement says any. AWS/Python is covered by adjacency on one half: Python is hands-on (Spark ETL pipelines), and AWS is absent but Azure is squarely adjacent — same capability, different vendor, which is what the rubric's PostgreSQL-for-MySQL rule admits, and it is the mirror image of the Azure-vs-AWS call made at train 3590. BI Tools is the one absent determination and it pins the band: the JD marks it 'a must', and no BI tool appears anywhere — not Periscope, Sisense or Tableau, and not Power BI, Looker or Qlik either. This is a genuinely well-tooled resume that lists its stack exhaustively by category, so the absence is informative rather than an oversight. One absent → band 1, notwithstanding four covered items.

Deep experience and understanding of SQL (MySQL, Postgres, Redshift)

experience_level.notes: JD asks a minimum of 5 years of data engineering. Role-matching is direct rather than inferred, and the seniority trajectory (ETL Developer to Lead to Senior Data Engineer) matches a Data Engineer II posting comfortably. Evidenced years exceed the floor by roughly 3.3 years → 5. The Summary's '9 years of Industry experience' self-claim is excluded on principle and is not needed; the dated segments carry the floor on their own.

education_domain_fit.notes: Bachelor of Engineering in Computer Science & Information Technology (Sri Krishnadevaraya University, 2011). The JD states no degree requirement — its Qualifications block is entirely skills and years — so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level technical education for a Data Engineer II. Level meets it. Field is computer science, squarely within the data engineering occupation; this is the core discipline for the role, not an adjacent one. Both halves fit → 5. Consistency note: this scores 5 where train 3590's Business Analytics master's scored 3 and train 596's Computer Applications bachelor's scored 3 — computer science is the core field for data engineering, while business analytics and applications computing are related-rather-than-core. Same rule, different field values.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's 'All candidates must meet the qualifications below' block. Met: the 5-year data engineering floor, data marts and modeling, deep SQL, and ETL tooling — each reusing a hands-on determination. AWS/Python = indeterminate: the determination is covered, so the unmet rule does not reach it, but the AWS half rests entirely on Azure adjacency while the JD marks AWS required and describes a warehouse built on Redshift, Airflow and Sisense — whether a hiring manager accepts an Azure engineer against an explicitly AWS stack is a call a reasonable annotator could make either way. Recorded as the same judgment made at train 3590 in the opposite direction, so the two labels stay consistent on cloud-vendor transferability. BI Tools = unmet: the JD says 'is a must', no BI tool of any vendor appears, and the resume's exhaustive category-by-category skills listing means the silence is evidence rather than omission — clearly unmet, not indeterminate. One clear unmet → 0 — four met items and a strong 8-year record buy nothing, the ledger has no ratio; soft veto fires (cap + gate).

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement, and a close one worth the owner's eye: the dataset says Good Fit; the rubric returns 3.0 with an unmet veto. Unlike train 5063 or train 3861, the dataset's label is defensible here — this IS a strong data engineer for a data engineering job, with 8.3 years, deep ETL, modeling and SQL, and the whole disagreement rests on two gaps: no BI tool, and Azure instead of AWS. The rubric gates rather than rejects, so the two verdicts are closer than the numbers suggest. boundary recorded for exactly that reason: a screener could defensibly advance this candidate on the strength of the core data engineering match, or reject on the two stated musts, and the rubric routes the call to a human rather than deciding it. Recorded judgment, and it is the one to review: the whole veto rests on the BI Tools item. It is the JD's least central requirement — the role is described as building pipelines, ETLs and data models, with reporting handled through Sisense by others — yet 'is a must' is unambiguous language, and the rubric's ledger has no ratio and no weighting, so the least important must sinks the pair exactly as hard as the most important one would. The alternative reading, that BI tools are peripheral and should be indeterminate rather than unmet, was rejected because the JD's language does not support it and because inventing a centrality judgment would import precisely the ratio the ledger forbids. But it does show the cost of the no-ratio design on a pair like this, and it pairs with the train 4160 observation about the geometry compressing strong partial matches into low bands.

skills_coverage.notes: Three stated skills musts, all partial, none absent and none fully covered — the cleanest example in the batch of the partial cap doing exactly what it was designed for. Each requirement pairs a general practice the candidate genuinely has with a specific Agile/product discipline she does not. Requirement solicitation and decomposition: solicitation is hands-on and repeated (report requirements gathered from stakeholders, cross-functional departments and business users; business partners engaged to identify information needs; Business Requirement Descriptions documented), but decomposition techniques — breaking epics into features, stories and acceptance criteria, which is the JD's core ask — have no evidence at all. Product Development and SDLC understanding: working inside 'a structured Waterfall and Agile project methodology approach' is adjacent to SDLC fluency, but product development is absent; this candidate builds reports and dashboards on products rather than developing them. Agile Scrum: Agile appears verbatim, so the requirement is not absent, but Scrum specifically does not — no sprints, no ceremonies, no backlog, no refinement, no Scrum team anywhere in nine years of dated duties. Three partials, no absent → band 2 (partial caps at 2). Stance A excludes the entire Preferred Qualifications block, and it costs her heavily here: Power BI, Azure DevOps, data-heavy products and financial-services industry experience are all preferred, and she has strong claims on several — Tableau throughout, Snowflake and Redshift warehouses, financial and retail industry work. None of it can move the band.
