Proficient level of understanding of Product Development and Software Development Life Cycles
Solid experience with Agile Scrum methodology

experience_level.notes: JD asks 5+ years of BA experience for a Senior Business Analyst on a Scrum team. Dated segments are well-formed: Data Analyst 03/2013–07/2014 (~1.3 yrs), Sr Data Analyst 01/2016–07/2017 (~1.5 yrs), BI Consultant/Sr Data Analyst 08/2017–01/2019 (~1.4 yrs), Lead BI Developer/Principal Data Analyst 01/2019–current (~5.4 yrs) — roughly 9.6 years, comfortably past the numeric floor, with a clear seniority trajectory to Lead/Principal. The question is role-matching, and the answer is partial: every title is BI Developer or Data Analyst, never Business Analyst, but the duties carry real BA content — requirements gathered from stakeholders, business requirements identified with business partners, requirement documentation maintained, solutions presented to clients. Under the role-matching rule those duties do match, so the years accrue; what does not accrue is the specific work this JD centres on — epic decomposition, story writing, backlog refinement, facilitating agile ceremonies, sharing Scrum Master duties. Clear relevant experience in role-matching segments with a real gap in the nature and seniority of the BA work itself → 3. The Summary's '7+ years of extensive IT experience' self-claim is excluded and, as at train 3148, understates what the dated segments evidence.

education_domain_fit.notes: Master's in Computer Science (3.6) and a B.Tech in Electronics and Communication Engineering (3.3). The Required Qualifications block names no degree, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level education for a Senior Business Analyst sitting inside a Product Engineering practice, on a Scrum team, working on a data analytics product. Level is met and exceeded. Field fits squarely — computer science is core to a technical BA role of this kind, where the work is decomposing software epics and translating business concepts for a development team, and the master's is the degree that governs here rather than the electronics bachelor's. Both halves fit → 5. Consistency: this matches train 3773 (CS + MBA, technical BA, 5) and sits above train 935 (Electrical Engineering, technical BA, 3), the difference being that computer science is core to software requirements work while electrical engineering is merely related.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the Required Qualifications block, and nothing in it is clearly unmet — the first pair in the set where the ledger reaches indeterminate without a single unmet item on the skills side. Met: grasping business concepts and translating them to a development team (complex business problems analyzed into insight for decision-makers, solutions presented to clients). Indeterminate ×4. 5+ years of BA: ~9.6 dated years carrying real requirements duties, but never under a BA title and never doing this JD's kind of BA work — judgeable either way. Requirement solicitation and decomposition: solicitation strong, decomposition absent. Product Development and SDLC: methodology exposure adjacent, product development absent. Agile Scrum: Agile named, Scrum absent — and this is the one that most deserves a human, because the JD wants a BA who can share Scrum Master duties and run refinement sessions, and 'worked using an Agile project methodology approach' neither confirms nor refutes that. No item clearly unmet, four indeterminate → 3; the soft veto fires in its indeterminate mode → force_gate only, no aggregate cap. Excluded as dispositional boilerplate, per the standing stance: excellent analytical and critical thinking skills, exceptional learning agility, excellent communication skills, demonstrated ability to work collaboratively, proactive self-starter with personal accountability, and high degree of organization — six of the eleven Required Qualifications lines, which is worth noting in itself.

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement, but a soft one: the dataset says Good Fit, the rubric returns 2.9 with an indeterminate veto — meaning the rubric does not reject this candidate, it asks a human to check her Scrum experience. That is a narrower gap than the numbers imply, and the dataset's label is defensible: she is a senior, technically-credentialed analyst with nine years of stakeholder requirements work applying to a BA role. boundary recorded because the call genuinely rests on one question — whether 'Agile project methodology' in a BI reporting context means she can run refinement sessions and share Scrum Master duties on a product team. Recorded observation about this JD: it is 7,669 characters and its Required Qualifications block is eleven lines, six of which are pure dispositional boilerplate (learning agility, communication, collaboration, self-starter, organization, critical thinking) that the ledger discards. So a very long posting yields three scorable skills musts, all of them phrased as general understandings rather than named tools — which is why all three land on partial rather than resolving cleanly either way. This is the third structural note in the batch about JDs that bury their real requirements: train 935 was too terse to state them, train 3773 filed them under Preferred, and this one dilutes them with boilerplate. Common thread for the P0 report: stance A plus the must/preferred distinction assume JDs are well-structured documents, and in this corpus they frequently are not.

skills_coverage.notes: Two stated skills musts, both drawn from requirement language embedded in the Responsibilities block ('Demonstrate good working knowledge and experience with ...', 'Must have strong knowledge and comprehension of ...'); the JD's separate mention of 'Reading and understanding of schematics and wire lists' restates the second and is merged rather than double-listed. The instrumentation item is partial: computers are evidenced, and measurement instrumentation is genuinely hands-on but of a different kind — NDT ultrasound and MPI, shaft laser alignment, oil sample analysis, condition monitoring, Caterpillar VIMs/ET/TT — which is condition-monitoring instrumentation rather than the electrical bench the JD describes; digital multimeters and pneumatic/hydraulic gauges are both absent. Some components present, some absent → partial. The schematics item is absent on the majority rule, and it is the decisive one: schematics, block diagrams, wire lists and functional diagrams are four coordinate components and not one of them appears anywhere in the resume — no drawing, print or diagram work in any dated segment. Only the last two components have anything: basic electronics is adjacent via the electrical engineering degree and 'basic electrical troubleshooting trainings', and the VDC/VAC systems knowledge is at best adjacent via a Power-concentration degree and motor sizing/overload audit work. Four of six components absent → majority-absent → absent. One absent → band 1. Excluded from the band: 'Electrical Engineering Degree Desirable' is preferred (stance A) and is a degree item in any case, so it belongs to education_domain_fit.

determinations.requirement: Working knowledge and experience with computers, data recorders, digital multimeters, various measurement instruments, pneumatic/hydraulic gauges
Strong knowledge of schematics, block diagrams, wire lists and functional diagrams, plus basic Electronics and operation of 12VDC-72VDC and 24VAC-480VAC systems

experience_level.notes: JD asks a minimum of 5 years in a related field, and parenthesises what it means by related: electrical testing. Dated segments are well-formed: Electrical Engineering Intern 11/2015–01/2016 (~0.2 yr), Graduate Trainee Engineer 05/2018–08/2018 (~0.25 yr), Maintenance Planner 10/2018–10/2019 (~1 yr), Reliability Engineer 11/2019–current (~4.5 yrs) — about 6 years total, which clears the floor numerically. Role-matching is where it fails. The only segment that is electrical testing is the three-month internship (assisting senior engineers with product testing, basic electrical troubleshooting, calibrating electrical systems) — that is a real role-matching dated segment, so band 1 does not apply, but it is roughly 0.2 years against a 5-year floor. The current Reliability Engineer role does substantial testing — NDT, condition monitoring, test data creation, test result analysis, technical test reports — but it is asset-reliability and mechanical testing, adjacent to electrical testing rather than an instance of it, and the Maintenance Planner year is scheduling rather than testing at all. Clear relevant experience in a role-matching segment with a very large real gap → 3. As at train 2980, the band does not scale with gap size: a ~4.8-year shortfall bands identically to train 3559's ~1.2-year shortfall, and the veto is what separates them.

education_domain_fit.notes: Bachelor of Electrical Engineering with a Power concentration (Papua New Guinea University of Technology, 2018), plus a Grade 12 School Certificate in Science, Physics & Advanced Maths. The JD states a High School Diploma as its required level and names an Electrical Engineering degree as desirable, so the stated floor is cleared outright and the role-implied ceiling is met exactly — this candidate holds precisely the degree the posting says it would like. Field is electrical engineering, squarely within the electrical engineering occupation, and the Power concentration is directly relevant to a role operating 24VAC-480VAC systems. Both halves fit → 5. Note the preferred-block degree is used here as evidence of the role-implied level, not imported as a must — the same handling as train 400 — and stance A's exclusion of preferred items applies to skills_coverage's band, not to this dimension's reading of what level the role implies. This is the fifth pair in the batch where a top-band education sits beside a bottom-band skills score.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's Educational Requirements and Essential Functions, plus the requirement language embedded in Responsibilities. High School Diploma or Equivalent = met (a Bachelor of Electrical Engineering exceeds it outright). 5 years electrical testing = unmet (~0.2 years of role-matching electrical testing evidenced, reusing the experience_level determination). Schematics/block diagrams/wire lists/functional diagrams with basic electronics and VDC/VAC systems = unmet (majority-absent bundle, reusing the skills_coverage determination) — the JD marks this one 'Must have' explicitly. Instrumentation = indeterminate: real hands-on measurement work of an adjacent kind, with two named components absent — a partial that a reasonable annotator could judge either way. Two clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Excluded from the ledger and worth stating, since this JD has more of them than most: 'Must be able to work different shifts and overtime as needed' is availability, 'Must be able to lift 50 lbs' is a physical requirement — neither sits in the rubric's ledger categories (skills, years, degrees, certifications, licenses, visas, location) and neither is resume-evidenceable, so both are out on the same stance applied to dispositional boilerplate throughout this batch. 'Technician must understand and apply all applicable ALSTOM Safety Procedures located on the PRISMA database' is also excluded, on a different ground: it is a post-hire obligation rather than a hiring qualification — no candidate can evidence knowledge of a specific employer's internal safety database before joining, so ledgering it would fire an indeterminate veto on every applicant alive.

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement: dataset says Good Fit, rubric returns 2.4 with an unmet veto. The dataset's reading is comprehensible — this is an electrical engineering graduate with six years of hands-on industrial engineering, testing and troubleshooting, applying to an electrical testing contract, and the profession matches. The rubric's reading is narrower and, on stated requirements, defensible: the posting wants a test technician who reads schematics and wire lists and works 12VDC-72VDC and 24VAC-480VAC systems on rail vehicles, and the resume evidences none of that — its testing is reliability-oriented (NDT, oil analysis, condition monitoring) and its electrical work stops at a 2015 internship. Both readings are honest; they are answering different questions, which is by now the recurring theme of this batch. boundary recorded because the call really could go either way: an EE degree holder with a Power concentration and six years of engineering could very plausibly do this job, and the missing items are the kind a competent engineer picks up quickly — but they are also exactly what the JD asks for and the document does not show them. Recorded ledger-scope note the owner may want to ratify: this JD is the first in the sample to state physical and shift-availability requirements ('lift 50 lbs', 'different shifts and overtime'), and the ALSTOM/PRISMA line is the first post-hire obligation dressed as a 'must'. All three were excluded on principle rather than judged, and the rubric's scope_notes do not address any of them; blue-collar and technician postings in this corpus will carry more of these, so a rule may be worth stating.

skills_coverage.notes: Two stated skills musts. MS Office is covered at keyword level ('Proficient in Excel, PowerPoint and Word'). The other is absent on the majority rule, and it decides the band: 'fair credit practices, accounting principles and collection regulations' is three coordinate components, and only the middle one lands. Accounting principles are genuinely there — a BS in Accounting, CPA-exam eligibility, nine years of tax accounting, 'extensive knowledge of accounting software and processes' — though evidenced adjacently, through tax practice rather than the revenue-recognition principles the JD's ASC 606 duties describe. Fair credit practices and collection regulations are both absent outright: nothing in this resume touches credit, collections or receivables, and tax accounting is not adjacent to either — they are consumer-credit and debt-collection compliance domains, a different body of regulation entirely. Two of three components absent → majority-absent → absent → band 1. Excluded by stance A: the industry preference clause riding on the same sentence ('preference to those who have billing and revenue experience within software andor fintech industries') and 'NetSuite andor Salesforce experience preferred' — the latter costs nothing here, since neither is evidenced. Excluded as dispositional boilerplate: excellent verbal and written communication, excellent interpersonal skills, strong time management, ability to adapt to changing priorities, exceptional analytical aptitude, maintaining confidentiality, and making recommendations using sound judgment — seven of the JD's twelve Requirements lines.

determinations.requirement: Proficiency in fair credit practices, accounting principles and collection regulations
Excellent knowledge of MS Office

experience_level.notes: The JD's years ask is specific about the role, not just the field: 5 years 'as a revenue specialist billing and revenue recognition'. Dated segments: Runner 05/2011–07/2011 (~0.2 yr), Leasing Assistant 07/2012–02/2013 (~0.6 yr), Lead Tax Intern 2014–03/2014 (malformed — see hesitations), International Tax Accountant 2015–current (~9 yrs). The nine-year segment is substantial and is unambiguously accounting, but its duties are international tax: preparing 5471, 8858, 8865, 1120-F and IC-DISC returns, M&A permanent-establishment due diligence, international tax research. Not one duty in any segment is billing, revenue recognition, invoicing, receivables or ASC 606. Under the role-matching rule, tax accounting does not accrue years to a revenue-recognition requirement — the same logic that stopped train 3978's OBIEE years accruing to a Java floor, and the rubric's own 'Java years accrue in a Java Developer role' example. No role-matching dated segments → band 1. Recorded, because this is the closest call of its kind in the batch: the occupation matches (accounting) while the sub-discipline does not, which is a narrower gap than train 596's QA-for-data-engineering or train 3861's sales-for-DevOps. The rubric offers no sub-discipline tier between 'role-matching' and not, so a tax accountant scores the same 1 as a candidate from an unrelated profession — arguably too blunt, and worth the owner's attention alongside the train 4160 and 6220 notes on band compression.

education_domain_fit.notes: Bachelor of Science in Accounting (University of Texas at Dallas, 2014, GPA 2.9), with a note of eligibility to sit for the CPA Examination. The JD states the requirement twice — once in its Requirements block and again under Education/Certifications — asking a Bachelor's in Finance or Accounting or a related discipline. This is that degree exactly: level met, field not merely adjacent but the named discipline itself, squarely within the accounting occupation → 5. The second education entry (University of Texas at Austin, May 2012) has a blank degree and blank field, reading 'to:The University of Texas at Austin' — it evidences nothing and is not scored; the Dallas accounting degree carries this dimension on its own. CPA eligibility is a note about a future exam rather than a held certification, so it is not treated as one.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's Requirements block. Bachelor's in Finance/Accounting = met (a BS in Accounting is the named discipline, reusing the education_domain_fit determination). MS Office = met. English proficiency = met (nine years of US tax practice, technical research and documentation in English — this is a language requirement rather than one of the scope_notes categories, but it is checkable from the document and evidenced, so it is ledgered rather than excluded as boilerplate). 5 years as a revenue specialist in billing and revenue recognition = unmet (zero role-matching years, reusing the experience_level determination). Fair credit practices / accounting principles / collection regulations = unmet (majority-absent bundle, reusing the skills_coverage determination). Two clear unmet → 0 — three met items buy nothing, the ledger has no ratio; soft veto fires (cap + gate).

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement, and a substantial one: the dataset says Good Fit; the rubric returns 1.8 with an unmet veto. The dataset's reading is that an accountant with an accounting degree is a good fit for an accounting job, which at profession level is true. The rubric's reading is that the posting asks for five years of revenue-recognition and billing specialism plus fair-credit and collections knowledge, and this candidate is an international tax specialist with none of it. This is the same shape as train 5063 — dataset tracking profession and calibre, rubric tracking stated requirements — but it is the most sympathetic instance of it in the batch, because the distance here is one sub-discipline rather than one profession, and a revenue-accounting team could plausibly retrain a tax accountant in months. Recorded rather than softened: the rubric scores evidence, and there is no revenue-recognition evidence to score. anomaly recorded for malformed dates and an empty degree field: the Lead Tax Intern segment reads '2014to03/2014' — a range whose start year is not earlier than its end date, so its duration is zero or negative and cannot be counted; the International Tax Accountant segment starts at a bare '2015' with no month; and the second education entry has no degree and no field at all. Less severe than the '01/1' corruption in train 2189, 175, 970, 1089 and 3229, but the same class of defect, and it means even the years this candidate does have cannot be counted precisely. Also worth noting for the P0 report: at 1,763 characters this is by far the shortest resume in the sample (the mean is ~6,100), which gives every dimension a thin evidentiary base.

skills_coverage.notes: The JD states its requirements in an unusually explicit structured table — years, Required/Preferred, skill — and marks four at 8 years Required: Business Analyst experience, SQL, System Reporting (Oracle, SQL), and Systems Testing (Unit, Integration, UAT). A fifth must, 'Must have experience working in an Agile environment', is stated in the body. Every one is absent against this resume. The candidate is a public health researcher: the tooling is STATA, R, SAS, GIS and NVIVO, the work is epidemiology — FISH techniques for DNA abnormalities, bio-assays for wastewater estrogenicity, statistical models from field data, epidemiologic study design. SQL appears nowhere and nothing is adjacent to it: STATA and SAS are statistical packages, not relational query languages, and reading them as adjacent would stretch adjacency past transferability into 'both involve data'. System reporting against Oracle, systems testing at unit/integration/UAT level, and Agile are absent without any near-miss at all. Business analyst experience is absent too — the closest content is 'Overseeing research proposals, grants, and manuscript preparation', which shares the word grants with the JD's Grants Management application but is grant-writing rather than business analysis of a grants system. All five absent → band 0, the all-absent case. Excluded by stance A: the table's Preferred rows (Agile at 4 years, Project/Product Management at 2, Jira at 1). Excluded as dispositional: 'Excellent communication skills (verbal and in writing)', notwithstanding that the table marks it Required with an 8-year quantity — see hesitations.

determinations.requirement: Business Analyst experience (8 years, Required)
SQL experience (8 years, Required)
System Reporting (Oracle, SQL) (8 years, Required)
Systems Testing (Unit, Integration, UAT) (8 years, Required)
Experience working in an Agile environment

experience_level.notes: The JD asks four stacked 8-year floors. Dated segments: Graduate Teaching Assistant 08/2012–12/2015 (~3.3 yrs), Environmental Health and Safety Intern 06/2013–08/2013 (~0.2 yr), Data Analyst Consultant 09/2014–01/2015 (~0.3 yr), Research Assistant 06/2014–present (~10 yrs) — the segments overlap heavily, which is plausible for concurrent academic and assistantship work but makes any total unreliable. It does not matter: every segment is public health research, epidemiology, laboratory work or teaching, and not one contains a business analysis, SQL, system reporting or systems testing duty. Zero role-matching dated segments for any of the four floors → band 1. Note the 'Data Analyst Consultant' title is the only one that even gestures at the JD, and its duties are environmental contaminant research and wastewater pollution control — a title match with no duty match, which is precisely what the role-matching rule exists to catch.

education_domain_fit.notes: BS in Biology (Tougaloo College, 2010), MS in Public Health (Tulane, 2012), and a Doctor of Public Health in progress (George Washington University) whose field is blank and whose date reads 'Expected inPresent'. The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level business or IT education for a Business Analyst working on a grants management application. Level is met several times over — this is the most highly educated candidate in the sample, with a doctorate underway. Field is unrelated: biology and public health are not business analysis, information systems or a related discipline, and neither is adjacent to systems requirements work. Field unrelated → 1, the same reading applied to train 3559's BA in Art and train 3861's Sociology/International Economics. Level fitting while field is unrelated again sits between band 1's 'Neither fits' headline and its disjunctive criteria text; the criteria text governs, as throughout this batch. Industry note: the JD's grants domain sits in health and human services, and this candidate's public health education is squarely in that vertical — but industry verticals are excluded from this dimension by owner decision (option A), and the occupation here is business analysis, to which public health is unrelated. The same exclusion was applied to train 1089's MBA in Finance against a financial-applications testing role.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's Minimum Requirements table and its body musts. All five unmet, each reusing a skills_coverage determination: Business Analyst experience, SQL, System Reporting, Systems Testing, and Agile. Texas location = indeterminate: the JD marks it under OTHER SPECIAL REQUIREMENTS ('Remote work Mon - Fri, Location: within the state of Texas'), location sits in the rubric's ledger categories, and the resume states no candidate location — only employer and university locations across New Jersey, Louisiana, Washington DC and Oregon, none of which is the candidate's address. Cannot decide, so recorded rather than inferred, consistent with train 1050, 3861 and 4160. Five clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Excluded as availability boilerplate: 'Some on-call support may be required' and 'may be required to work outside the normal business hours on weekends, evenings and holidays'.

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement of the same severity as train 3861, and in the same direction: the dataset says GOOD FIT; the rubric returns 0.5 — its joint-lowest aggregate — with five clearly unmet required skills. A public health epidemiologist with no SQL is not a good fit for a Business Analyst contract demanding 8 years of SQL, 8 years of Oracle system reporting and 8 years of systems testing; there is no reading of this posting under which she is, and unlike train 5699 the gap is not one sub-discipline but an entire profession. Together with train 3861 (VP of Sales labeled Potential Fit for an Ansible developer role) and train 970 (a well-matched data analyst labeled No Fit), this is now the third sample where the dataset label appears simply wrong rather than merely differently-motivated. That is a material finding: the dataset's three classes cannot serve as a base layer for the layered ground truth without human review, and the P0 report should say so with these three rows as evidence. anomaly recorded for malformed education data: the doctorate entry reads 'Doctor of Public Health:,Expected inPresent-' with a blank field and a date of 'Present', and a fourth education entry ('Biology:Health Science, Expected in May 2012, Tulane') appears to duplicate the MS with degree and field transposed. The document also carries PDF-extraction hyphenation artifacts ('devel- oped', 'conserva- tion'). Corpus artifacts noted but not flagged, per the standing line: the Summary opens 'Highly motivated Sales Associate with extensive customer service and sales experience' — the identical boilerplate seen atop train 5063 and train 2980, here on an epidemiologist's resume — and the employer substitution is at its most incongruous, attributing wastewater and environmental contaminant research to C.H. Robinson Worldwide, a logistics company. Recorded JD-side contradiction: the body says 'Must have experience working in an Agile environment' while the requirements table lists Agile as Preferred at 4 years. The body's explicit must-language was taken as governing, and it costs nothing here since Agile is absent either way, but a JD that contradicts itself about what is required is worth noting — as is its demand for 8 years of 'Excellent communication skills', a quantity that makes the Required table's own categories suspect.

skills_coverage.notes: The strongest technical match in the sample, and the band still lands at 2 — worth reading carefully. Six stated skills musts; five are covered with hands-on evidence, several of them emphatically. Databricks, which the JD singles out as the platform the job requires expertise in, is hands-on: large data-set migrations to Databricks, cluster creation and administration, pipelines configured, ADLS Gen2 loads via ADF, Databricks notebooks authored for business use cases. Spark is hands-on and deep (Spark Context, Spark-SQL, Dataframe API, Spark Streaming, MLlib, Pair RDDs, PySpark and Scala, plus performance optimization). Big data technologies are covered across the whole enumeration — Hadoop hands-on, NoSQL across MongoDB/HBase/Cassandra/DynamoDB, MPP via Teradata/Redshift/Synapse, and OLTP/OLAP via transactional and dimensional modeling. Python, Scala and Java are all three present against a requirement asking for any one. Cloud is hands-on on two of the three named providers (AWS extensively, Azure extensively). The development-tools item is the sole partial and it caps the band at 2: CI/CD is hands-on (pipelines built and maintained, Jenkins listed), orchestration and automation are strong (Airflow, Oozie, Terraform, Ansible, Step Functions), REST API is present (Django REST framework, RESTful services, API routes), BI tools are present (Tableau, Power BI, SSRS), SQL interfaces are present (Spark SQL, SnowSQL, a Phoenix SQL layer on HBase) — but unit and integration testing has no evidence at all. Five of six components present, one absent → partial → band 2. Recorded: this is the same mechanism as train 3590's NoSQL partial, and it is the batch's clearest demonstration that the partial cap is a hard cap rather than a penalty — one missing component in one of six requirements holds a near-perfect technical match to the same band as train 3559's Excel-only accountant. The geometry is being followed, not misapplied, but the owner should see what it costs here. Excluded by stance A: the Data Science/ML Engineer block ('desired'), which costs this candidate real credit — he has SageMaker, MLflow-adjacent workflows, MLlib and model deployment.

determinations.requirement: Expertise in Databricks
Hands-on and technical expertise with Apache Spark
Design and implementation in big data technologies including Hadoop, NoSQL, MPP, OLTP, OLAP
Comfortable programming in Python, Scala, or Java
Using and designing solutions on cloud infrastructure and services such as AWS, Azure, or GCP
Development tools for CI/CD, unit and integration testing, automation and orchestration, REST API, BI tools, and SQL interfaces

experience_level.notes: JD asks 5+ years as a Software or Data Engineer doing query tuning, performance tuning, troubleshooting and debugging of Spark or other big data solutions. Dated segments: Python Developer 09/2012–10/2013 (~1.1 yrs), Big Data Engineer/Hadoop Developer 10/2013–12/2015 (~2.2 yrs), Data Engineer 01/2016–11/2019 (~3.9 yrs), AWS Data Engineer 01/2022–02/2022 (~0.1 yr). The three engineering segments before the gap total roughly 7.2 years, all role-matching: every one carries the exact duties named — Spark performance optimization, Hive query tuning via partitioning and bucketing, HQL optimization, database performance monitoring and tuning. Evidenced years exceed the 5-year floor in role-matching segments → 5. Two oddities recorded rather than scored, since neither is a years-or-seniority question the rubric asks: there is a gap from 11/2019 to 01/2022, and the most recent segment lasts one month (01/2022–02/2022), which is either a very short engagement or a data defect. The Summary's 'around 7 years' self-claim is excluded on principle and happens to agree with the dated evidence.

education_domain_fit.notes: Master of Science in Data Engineering (George Mason University, 2023) and a Bachelor of Engineering in Computer Networking and Telecommunications (University of Mumbai, 2019). The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied: bachelor's-level technical education for a Solution Architect in data engineering. Level is met and exceeded. Field could not fit more exactly — the master's is literally in Data Engineering, the occupation named in the job title, so this is core rather than adjacent; the networking bachelor's is related and adds nothing either way. Both halves fit → 5. Recorded inconsistency, not scored: the education dates do not reconcile with the work history — the bachelor's is dated 2019 and the master's 2023, while the work history begins with a Python Developer role in 09/2012, so the candidate would have been working as an engineer for seven years before earning a bachelor's. One of the two timelines is wrong. The rubric scores degree level and field, not chronology, so this does not move the band, but it is the kind of internal contradiction a human screener would catch and an evidence-citing agent should surface.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the Required Skills block. Met: Databricks expertise (the JD's headline requirement, hands-on), Apache Spark, big data technologies across Hadoop/NoSQL/MPP/OLTP/OLAP, Python/Scala/Java (the or-disjunction satisfied three times over), cloud infrastructure design on AWS and Azure, and the 5+ year Software/Data Engineer floor with its query- and performance-tuning duties — every one reusing a hands-on determination. Customer-facing pre-sales/post-sales/architecture-guidance/consulting = met: the requirement is a four-way disjunction and the consulting arm carries it — the candidate's engineering was delivered from Infosys and Deloitte, both consultancies, on client engagements, with duties collaborating with solution architects on engagement strategies for key accounts. Development tools = indeterminate: the determination is partial, so the unmet rule does not reach it, but the JD asks for unit and integration testing experience and the document evidences none — genuinely judgeable either way for a candidate whose CI/CD, orchestration and API tooling is otherwise strong. No item clearly unmet, one indeterminate → 3; the soft veto fires in its indeterminate mode → force_gate only, no aggregate cap. Excluded as dispositional: 'Excellent communication skills and comfortable presenting to strategic stakeholders/leadership' and 'Passionate about learning new technologies and making customers successful'.

hesitations: The rubric and the dataset agree, and this is the best-matched pair in the sample: dataset says Good Fit; the rubric returns 3.5 with only an indeterminate veto — no rejection, just a check. It is also the most useful counterweight to the disagreement cases (train 3861, 4928, 5699), because it shows the rubric does recognise a strong match when the evidence is there; the divergences elsewhere are not a systematic severity bias. boundary recorded because the one open question — whether a data engineer with no evidenced unit/integration testing suits a post-sales Solution Architect role — is a fair thing to ask a human, alongside the softer question of whether a hands-on engineer wants a customer-facing architect job at all. Recorded judgment on the item that decides the band: the development-tools requirement was read as a coordinate bundle ('Development Tools for CICD, Unit and Integration testing, Automation and Orchestration, REST API, BI tools, and SQL Interfaces'), which makes it partial and caps the dimension at 2. Reading it instead as a head skill ('Development Tools') with the list exemplifying its range would make it covered and lift the band to 5 — every other determination is covered and hands-on. That is a two-band swing on one parsing call, and it is the sharpest instance of the bundle-vs-exemplification ambiguity flagged since train 3559. The coordinate reading was taken because the list enumerates six distinct tool categories joined by 'and', with 'E.g. Jenkins' supplying the exemplar separately — but the owner should rule on this, because the rule is not in the rubric and it is now demonstrably worth two bands on the heaviest-weighted dimension.

skills_coverage.notes: Fifteen stated skills musts, and the shape of the mismatch is stark: this JD wants a Software Engineering Manager who codes PHP and JavaScript, architects 3-tier web applications, and has done security, CMS, APIs, Docker and modern front-end work — and the resume is a mobile/device QA engineer. Ten determinations are absent with nothing adjacent: 3-tier and security architecture, building consumer-facing software at scale (this candidate tests software rather than builds it, which is a different skill, not an adjacent one), the entire security block, PHP and its frameworks, CMS systems, JavaScript/Node and its frameworks, API development and REST/OAuth2, web markup, Git/Mercurial/SVN (notably absent from an otherwise exhaustive tools list), and Docker. The database item is absent on the majority rule — SQL is present and relational is adjacent, but NoSQL and key-value are both missing, two of three components. Three items are covered, and all three are QA-shaped: writing unit, integration and end-to-end tests is hands-on and evidenced verbatim; testing frameworks is covered by adjacency (Appium and Selenium WebDriver for PHPUnit/Jest/Mocha — same category, different language); the Atlassian suite is covered via Jira at keyword level, the parenthetical exemplifying rather than listing three coordinate requirements. Change management and agile is partial — Agile and Scrum are evidenced, change management is not. Ten absent → band 1. Merged rather than double-listed, following the handling in train 901 and 3978: the JD's four separate security lines (vulnerabilities and mitigation, input validation/encryption/secure authentication, security and compliance, GDPR and accessibility standards) are recorded as one security requirement, PHP and PHP frameworks as one, and API development and REST/OAuth2 as one. Excluded by stance A: 'Experience with waterfall methodologies would be helpful'.

Building consumer-facing web and mobile software solutions at scale within the agile framework

Strong knowledge of PHP and PHP frameworks (Slim, Laravel, Symfony)
CMS systems such as WordPress, Drupal, Shopify, Squarespace, Joomla
JavaScript/Node and JavaScript frameworks (React/React Native, Sencha, Vue.js)

API development, including RESTful APIs using OAuth2/JWT tokens
Modern web markup: HTML5, CSS3, Babel, Webpack, TypeScript, LESS/SASS

Atlassian suite of tools (Jira, Confluence, Bitbucket)

experience_level.notes: Two stacked floors: 5+ years supervising or managing technical resources, and 10+ years of hands-on professional coding. All four work entries carry corrupt date fields — '01/1toPresent' for the current Software Test Engineer role and '01/1to01/1' for the other three — so no segment is dated and no years can be evidenced, the same corruption seen in train 2189, 175, 970, 1089 and 3229. Content is decisive independently. Against the coding floor there are no role-matching segments: every role is software verification and QA, and while Perl, Python and PowerShell are used, they are used to automate tests, not to build the web applications this role would own — test automation scripting is adjacent to professional coding, not an instance of it. Against the management floor there is likewise nothing: the candidate coordinated third-party and internal teams for field-test activities and trained other engineers, which is lead-adjacent, but no segment is a supervisory or management role. No role-matching dated segments for either floor → band 1. The Summary's '5+ years of work experience in Software QA and testing' is an undated self-claim, excluded, and it falls short of even the smaller of the two floors while describing the wrong discipline.

education_domain_fit.notes: MS in Information Technology and Management (Illinois Institute of Technology, 2011) and a BE in Electronics & Telecommunication (2008). The JD asks a BS/MS in Computer Science, Software Engineering, 'or related field' — so both level options are cleared, and the question is the field. Information Technology and Management is IT with a management orientation, and Electronics & Telecommunication is hardware engineering: both are related to software engineering, neither is core to it, and the 'or related field' clause is what carries them across the ledger threshold rather than making them a squarely-within fit. Level met + field related-rather-than-core → 3, not sub-graded. Consistent with train 596 (Computer Applications), train 1050 (Management Information Systems) and train 3590 (Business Analytics), all of which drew 3 on the same reading.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the Qualifications and Skills and Abilities blocks. Degree = met (an MS in IT and Management sits inside 'or related field' comfortably — the clause is broad, and this is not a borderline call needing indeterminate). Unit/integration/end-to-end tests = met (hands-on); Atlassian suite = met (Jira). Ten skills items unmet, each reusing an absent determination: 3-tier and security architecture, building consumer-facing software at scale, the security block, PHP and frameworks, CMS, JavaScript/Node, databases (majority-absent), API development, web markup, Git/Mercurial/SVN, and Docker. Both years floors unmet: 5+ years technical supervision and 10+ years hands-on coding, reusing the experience_level determination that no segment is role-matching for either. Indeterminate: change management and agile (partial — Agile evidenced, change management not), and testing frameworks (covered by adjacency alone — Appium and Selenium are real test frameworks, but PHPUnit, Jest and Mocha are the PHP/JavaScript unit-test frameworks this stack needs, and whether the transfer counts is judgeable either way). Twelve clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). Excluded as dispositional: 'Strong leadership, communication, organization, and problem-solving skills', 'Must be highly organized with the ability to effectively prioritize work', and 'Ability to influence and adapt to continuous improvement driven change'.

hesitations: Owner-vs-dataset disagreement, and a large one: the dataset says Good Fit; the rubric returns 1.4 with twelve clearly unmet requirements. A mobile-device QA engineer is not a good fit for a Software Engineering Manager role that asks for 10 years of hands-on coding, 5 years of management, strong PHP, Laravel, React, Docker, CMS platforms and 3-tier security architecture — he has none of the stack and neither of the floors. What plausibly drives the label is surface overlap the rubric correctly declines to credit: both documents are full of the words software, testing, agile, Scrum, SDLC, Jenkins and Jira, and the resume's testing depth genuinely does match two of the JD's fifteen skills lines — but they are the two lines about testing, on a posting for the person who would manage the engineers. This joins train 3861 (VP of Sales as Potential Fit for an Ansible role) and train 4928 (epidemiologist as Good Fit for a SQL business analyst role) as a sample where the dataset label appears simply wrong, and it is now the fourth Good Fit in this batch that the rubric places at or below 2.4. That pattern is the single most important finding for the P0 report: across the ten Good Fit pairs the dataset's label and the rubric's stated-requirement reading diverge far more often than they agree. anomaly recorded for the '01/1' date corruption across all four work entries, which is what forces experience_level to band 1 on evidentiary grounds before content is considered.

skills_coverage.notes: Three stated skills musts, all covered with hands-on evidence — the first pair in the sample to reach the top band. Strong programming: Python is hands-on across dated duties, alongside Java, JavaScript, TypeScript and Node.js, and the JD's parenthetical marks Golang/C++/Python as preferred languages rather than requiring them, so Python satisfies the head skill directly. Data analysis and troubleshooting: data quality monitoring for 4-5 data science teams, KPI dashboards built in Tableau and Grafana over millions of daily entries, ETL tables delivered for demand prediction models, and debugging owned during developer onboarding. Large-scale cloud applications with APIs and developer platforms: a GCP ingestion pipeline designed and deployed end to end (Pub/Sub, Dataflow, DataPrep, BigQuery, Stackdriver), a JavaScript SDK and ingest URL published with JWT authorization and Apigee as the API key management platform, and a monolith decoupled into Node.js/REST microservices. All covered, all hands-on → band 5. Excluded by stance A: the whole 'I'd Be Great If You Also Have These' block — IoT and sensors, knowledge of ML models and pipelines, large enterprise customers — and here the exclusion is generous rather than costly, since the candidate would partially cover it anyway (IoT device alerting and monitoring built, IoT device status ingested, 4 ML models supported, TensorFlow listed). See hesitations on what stance A also excludes in the other direction.

determinations.requirement: Strong programming skills (preferred Golang, C++ or Python)
Solid data analysis and troubleshooting skills
Experience developing large scale cloud applications using APIs and developer platforms

experience_level.notes: JD asks 4+ years of software development. Dated segments are well-formed: Programmer 03/2016–12/2018 (~2.8 yrs, designing and building an Auto-Test-System end to end) and Software Developer 12/2018–current (~5.5 yrs against the corpus's 2024 pin) — roughly 8.2 years, both role-matching, and the current segment is software development in substance (pipelines, SDKs, microservices, database design) with lead behaviour on top ('Led a team of 4 software developers', prototype demoed to director-level board members). Evidenced years roughly double the floor in role-matching segments → 5. As at train 3148 — the same resume, paired here against a different JD — the Summary's 'more than 4 years' self-claim understates the dated evidence, and the rubric's exclusion of self-claims helps rather than hurts this candidate.

education_domain_fit.notes: BS in Electrical, Electronics Engineering Technologies (Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology, 2011) and a Master of Science from Syracuse University (2014) whose field is blank — the entry reads 'Master of Science:,' with nothing between colon and comma. The JD asks a bachelor's in computer science or a related technical field involving software/systems engineering. Level is met by the bachelor's and exceeded by the master's whatever its field. Field is scored on the bachelor's, the only one the document identifies: electronics engineering technology is technical and related to software engineering, but not core to it. Level met + field related-rather-than-core → 3, not sub-graded. Identical to train 3148, which pairs this same resume against a Java/Spring Boot JD — the two labels agree, as they should, since the JD's education ask is materially the same in both.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the 'What You Should Have' block — and every item is met, which makes this the only pair in the sample where the soft veto does not fire. Degree = met, and doubly so: an Electronics Engineering Technologies bachelor's sits inside 'a related technical field involving software/systems engineering', and the JD's own disjunction offers 'or equivalent practical experience', which ~8 years of professional software development satisfies independently — so the degree half passes on either branch and this is not a borderline call needing indeterminate. 4+ years software development = met (~8.2 evidenced in role-matching segments). Strong programming skills = met; data analysis and troubleshooting = met; large-scale cloud applications with APIs = met — all three reusing hands-on skills_coverage determinations. No item unmet, none indeterminate → 5; the soft veto's on_met branch fires, which is to say it takes no action: no aggregate cap, no forced gate. gate_expected = false. Excluded as dispositional: 'Excellent communication skills'.

hesitations: The rubric and the dataset agree, and this is the only pair in all thirty where the veto reaches 'met' and no gate is forced — worth stating plainly, because the batch's running worry was that gate_expected might be constant. It is not: this label is the negative case the P1 gate config needs, and the sample now spans all three veto states (unmet ×24, indeterminate ×5, met ×1). Recorded observation the owner should weigh, because it cuts against the clean result: this role's actual work is machine learning and computer vision — 'design critical applications using machine learning and computer vision models' is the first line of About The Role — and the candidate has no computer vision evidence at all. But the JD files ML under 'I'd Be Great If You Also Have These', and computer vision appears nowhere in its requirements, so stance A excludes the former and the latter is simply not asked. The rubric therefore passes a candidate on a posting whose central technology it never scored. That is the same structural failure recorded at train 3773 (a JD whose real skills all sat in a Preferred block) and train 935 (a JD too terse to state its skills), arriving now at the opposite end: there it depressed a band, here it lifts one to 5 and clears the veto. It is the strongest argument in the batch for revisiting stance A or extending derived-musts — a JD's About The Role section is where this posting says what the job is, and the rubric is structurally blind to it. Also noted: this resume is the same document as train 4715's counterpart at train 3148, confirming that the corpus reuses resumes across pairs (the sample's seeding guarantees distinct JDs, not distinct resumes). The two labels are consistent where they should be — identical education band, identical experience band — and diverge only on skills, correctly, because the JDs ask for different things: Java/Spring Boot, which he has only as keywords, versus general programming and cloud APIs, which he has hands-on.

skills_coverage.notes: Seven stated skills musts, every one absent, with nothing adjacent to any of them. The JD wants Oracle, DataStage, UNIX, PL/SQL and expert SQL, plus cloud platforms, cloud data warehouses, cloud ETL/ELT tooling, data modelling, version control and Agile. The resume is a data entry clerk's: the tooling is Microsoft Excel, Access, Outlook and Adobe, and the duties are inputting client information into spreadsheets, correcting data entry errors, sorting source documents and proofreading. The near-miss worth naming explicitly is the vocabulary: this resume says 'database management', 'Data Management', 'Data Verification', 'Data Compilation', 'Data Review' and 'company database' repeatedly — but entering and verifying records in a company database is not adjacent to engineering one. Adjacency in this rubric means transferable capability, and nothing in data entry transfers to writing PL/SQL, building DataStage jobs or modelling a cloud warehouse; treating the shared word 'data' as adjacency would be a keyword collision of exactly the kind the rubric's evidence-of-use standard exists to reject. All seven absent → band 0, the all-absent case. Excluded by stance A: 'Good to have: AWS, Matillion, Snowflake' — which costs nothing, since all three are absent anyway.

determinations.requirement: Oracle, DataStage, UNIX, PL/SQL, SQL
Expert level experience with SQL
Experience with the cloud (AWS, Azure and/or Google Cloud Platform)
Experience in cloud-based data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Azure Synapse Analytics)
Experience with cloud-based ETL/ELT tools (Matillion, Glue, Data Factory) and data modelling
Experience with version control systems (Git, SVN)
Understanding of and willingness to embrace Agile Principles

experience_level.notes: The JD states no year count, only 'Data engineering experience', so proximity is judged against the role: a Data Engineer building Oracle/DataStage pipelines. Dated segments are well-formed: Crew Member 06/2016–08/2018 (~2.2 yrs), Cashier 06/2017–04/2018 (~0.9 yr, overlapping the previous), Manager 11/2018–09/2020 (~1.9 yrs), Data Entry Specialist 09/2020–current (~4 yrs) — about eight years of work, in fast food, retail cashiering, store management and data entry. Zero role-matching segments for data engineering: the closest, Data Entry Specialist, shares a word with the requirement and nothing else — its duties are keying records, error correction, document sorting and logbook completion. No role-matching dated segments → band 1.

education_domain_fit.notes: A High School Diploma (Idea Homeschool, Soldotna) is the entire education record — no degree, and the entry carries no date. The JD states no degree requirement, so the ask is role-implied, and for a Data Engineer working Oracle, PL/SQL, cloud warehouses and data modelling it clearly implies bachelor's-level technical education. This is the band-1 criteria's second disjunct exactly: 'no degree education where one is clearly expected'. Unlike the other band-1 education scores in this batch (train 3559's BA in Art, train 3861's Sociology, train 4928's Public Health), which failed on field while clearing level, this one fails on level itself and has no field to assess → 1. It is the only pair in the sample with no degree-level education at all.

hard_requirements.notes: Ledger over the JD's requirement list — all eight unmet, seven reusing skills_coverage determinations (Oracle/DataStage/UNIX/PL-SQL/SQL, expert SQL, cloud, cloud data warehouses, cloud ETL/ELT and data modelling, version control, Agile) and one reusing the experience_level determination (data engineering experience, no role-matching segments). Eight clear unmet → 0; soft veto fires (cap + gate). This is the most comprehensive ledger failure in the sample: not one item met, not one indeterminate — there is nothing here a reasonable annotator could argue about. Interpretation note, following train 1089: this JD is a recruiter's email and marks nothing with the words must or required, listing its requirements under 'Skills:' and a run of 'Experience with ...' lines. They are read as stated requirements on the same ground — where a posting's content is its requirements list, the list is the ledger, and the literal alternative would leave the ledger empty and hand a met veto to a candidate matching none of the JD.

hesitations: The most clear-cut mislabel in the sample: the dataset says GOOD FIT for a data entry clerk with a high school diploma against a Data Engineer contract requiring Oracle, DataStage, PL/SQL and expert SQL. The rubric returns 0.5 with eight unmet requirements and no indeterminates. There is no charitable reading — this is not the dataset tracking seniority (as at train 5063 or 3229), nor tracking profession (as at train 5699), because the candidate has neither the seniority nor the profession; the only thing the two documents share is the word 'data'. If the dataset's labels were generated by embedding similarity or keyword overlap, this row is what that failure mode looks like. This is now the fourth Good Fit in ten that the rubric places at or below 2.4, and the third sample (with train 3861 and train 4928) where the label appears simply wrong rather than differently-motivated. Recommend the owner treat these four rows as the evidence base for the P0 report's finding on dataset label quality. anomaly recorded for token-level corruption: the resume reads 'Adhered to sClairect data confidentiality policies', where the template name Claire has been substituted into the middle of the word 'strict' — the same 'Jessica Claire' template residue seen in train 2189's certifications block, here corrupting a word rather than appending a line. The education entry also has no date ('Expected in––'), and two employment segments overlap (Crew Member 06/2016–08/2018 and Cashier 06/2017–04/2018). Corpus artifact noted but not flagged, per the standing line: the employer fields are substituted to an absurd degree here — a fast-food crew role attributed to Marcus Corporation, cashiering to Kohler, a store manager role to Community Health System, and data entry to Sonic Healthcare USA.
